questions.rationale: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome causes CNS hyperstimulation: tremors, sneezing, and high-pitched crying.
choice_rationales: ✘ Sleepiness and bradycardia occur with CNS depression (e.g., from sedatives), not opioid withdrawal.
✔ NAS results from abrupt cessation of opioid exposure at birth, causing CNS hyperexcitability.
✘ Opioid-exposed neonates are typically small for gestational age, not large.
✘ High-pitched, shrill crying (not low-pitched) is characteristic of NAS due to neurological irritability.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Day 10 postpartum home visit — fundus not palpable abdominally — nurse interprets this as?
choices: A. Normal finding — uterus has returned to the true pelvis
questions.rationale: The fundus descends approximately 1 cm/day; by day 10 it is behind the symphysis pubis and non-palpable.
choice_rationales: ✔ Progressive involution means the uterus is expected to become non-palpable abdominally by day 9–10.
✘ Uterine atony presents with a boggy fundus and excess bleeding — the fundus IS palpable and soft.
✘ Retained fragments cause subinvolution with continued lochia and a higher-than-expected fundal position.
✘ Subinvolution means the uterus is NOT involuting normally; the fundus would still be palpable above the symphysis.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: FSH and LH in the menstrual cycle — correct student statement?
choices: A. Produced by ovaries to thicken endometrium
B. Released by anterior pituitary to stimulate follicles and trigger ovulation
D. Responsible for breast milk production
questions.rationale: FSH stimulates follicular development; LH surge triggers ovulation from the anterior pituitary.
choice_rationales: ✘ FSH and LH are gonadotropins from the anterior pituitary, not ovarian hormones; estrogen thickens the endometrium.
✔ FSH stimulates follicle growth and estrogen production; the LH surge triggers ovulation.
✘ FSH and LH are present throughout the reproductive years, not just during pregnancy.
✘ Prolactin from the anterior pituitary stimulates milk production, not FSH or LH.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Umbilical cord contents — correct description?
choices: A. One artery carrying oxygen and two veins carrying waste
B. Two arteries carrying waste and one vein carrying oxygen
C. Three identical vessels with oxygenated blood
D. Two veins and two arteries
questions.rationale: The single umbilical vein carries oxygenated blood to the fetus; two arteries carry deoxygenated blood to the placenta.
choice_rationales: ✘ This reverses the correct anatomy; arteries carry waste away, the vein carries oxygen to the fetus.
✔ AVA rule: Arteries (2) carry deoxygenated blood Away from the fetus; Vein (1) brings oxygen.
✘ The vessels carry blood in opposite directions with different oxygen content.
✘ The normal umbilical cord has 3 vessels (2 arteries, 1 vein); 4 vessels is abnormal.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: LMP October 10, 2025 — EDD by Naegele's Rule?
questions.rationale: Naegele's Rule: subtract 3 months (Oct→Jul), add 7 days (10+7=17), add 1 year (2025→2026).
choice_rationales: ✘ July 10 subtracts 3 months but forgets to add 7 days.
✔ October 10 − 3 months = July 10; + 7 days = July 17; + 1 year = July 17, 2026.
✘ June 17 incorrectly subtracts 4 months instead of 3.
✘ August 17 incorrectly subtracts only 2 months.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Second-trimester finding requiring immediate provider notification?
choices: B. Weight gain 1 pound last month
questions.rationale: FHR <110 bpm is bradycardia indicating potential fetal distress requiring immediate evaluation.
choice_rationales: ✘ FHR of 145 bpm is within the normal range (110–160 bpm).
✘ 1 lb/month weight gain is within the normal range for the second trimester.
✔ FHR of 100 bpm is below the normal range; bradycardia may indicate fetal hypoxia or cardiac abnormality.
✘ Occasional lower back pain is a common, normal complaint in pregnancy due to postural and hormonal changes.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Vaginal exam: midwife pushes up on lower uterine segment, fetus rises and falls back — term?
questions.rationale: Ballottement is a technique to detect a floating fetus by bouncing it against the examiner's fingers.
choice_rationales: ✘ Quickening is the mother's first perception of fetal movement, not an examiner's technique.
✔ Ballottement involves displacing the fetus and feeling it rebound — confirms a floating fetal mass in fluid.
✘ Hegar's sign is softening of the lower uterine segment, not a floating fetal technique.
✘ Lightning (engagement) is the descent of the fetal head into the pelvis near term.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Cervix has bluish-purple discoloration — nurse documents as?
questions.rationale: Chadwick's sign is blue-violet cervical/vaginal discoloration from increased pelvic vascularity in pregnancy.
choice_rationales: ✘ Goodell's sign is cervical softening (like a lips vs. nose consistency), not discoloration.
✔ The increased blood flow to pelvic organs in pregnancy causes the characteristic blue-purple color of Chadwick's sign.
✘ Hegar's sign is softening and compressibility of the lower uterine segment.
✘ Braxton Hicks are painless uterine contractions, not a cervical color change.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Rh-negative woman has spontaneous abortion at 11 weeks — RhoGAM?
choices: A. Necessary within 72 hours to prevent antibody formation
B. Not needed before second trimester
C. Only if prior blood transfusions
questions.rationale: Any Rh-positive fetal exposure (including early pregnancy loss) can sensitize an Rh-negative mother.
choice_rationales: ✔ RhoGAM must be given within 72 hours of any pregnancy loss to prevent Rh sensitization that could harm future pregnancies.
✘ Fetal Rh-positive RBCs are present from conception; sensitization risk exists from early pregnancy loss.
✘ Blood transfusion history is irrelevant; maternal exposure to fetal Rh+ blood is what determines the need.
✘ Delaying until the next pregnancy is too late; sensitization has already occurred.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 10 minutes post-delivery: boggy fundus displaced right of midline — first action?
questions.rationale: Bladder distention displaces the uterus and prevents contraction; emptying the bladder is the first step.
choice_rationales: ✘ Massage without first emptying the bladder will be ineffective; the full bladder is preventing contraction.
✘ Notifying the obstetrician may be needed but addressing the full bladder comes first.
✔ A displaced boggy fundus almost always indicates a full bladder; emptying it allows the uterus to contract and descend to midline.
✘ Increasing Pitocin without addressing the bladder distention will not achieve effective uterine contraction.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: HELLP syndrome — which finding is present?
questions.rationale: HELLP: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — elevated liver enzymes are a defining criterion.
choice_rationales: ✘ HELLP causes LOW platelets (thrombocytopenia), not high platelets.
✔ Elevated liver enzymes (AST, ALT) result from hepatic ischemia in HELLP syndrome.
✘ Bilirubin is ELEVATED in HELLP due to hemolysis, not decreased.
✘ Hemoglobin is DECREASED in HELLP due to microangiopathic hemolysis.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Lochia Rubra typically seen during which postpartum days?
questions.rationale: Lochia rubra (bloody red discharge) is the first stage, consisting mainly of blood and decidua from days 1–3.
choice_rationales: ✔ Lochia rubra is bright/dark red with blood clots, normal for the first 3 days postpartum.
✘ Days 4–10 describe lochia serosa — pinkish-brown, watery transition discharge.
✘ Days 11–21 describe lochia alba — creamy white or yellow, minimal discharge.
✘ After 6 weeks any discharge would be abnormal; lochia has ceased.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Late deceleration on fetal monitor — typically caused by?
questions.rationale: Late decelerations reflect fetal hypoxia from inadequate placental oxygen transfer during contractions.
choice_rationales: ✘ Head compression causes early decelerations (vagal response, benign pattern).
✘ Cord compression causes variable decelerations (abrupt onset, variable shape).
✔ Uteroplacental insufficiency reduces O₂ delivery during contractions; the fetal myocardium depresses late in the contraction, causing late decels.
✘ Fetal movement causes accelerations (reassuring), not late decelerations.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Magnesium sulfate antidote?
questions.rationale: Calcium gluconate is the physiological antagonist of magnesium, reversing CNS and respiratory depression.
choice_rationales: ✘ Protamine sulfate is the antidote for heparin overdose, not magnesium toxicity.
✔ Calcium ions compete with magnesium at cellular receptors; 1 g IV calcium gluconate reverses magnesium toxicity.
✘ Naloxone reverses opioid overdose, not magnesium toxicity.
✘ Epinephrine is used in anaphylaxis and cardiac arrest, not magnesium toxicity.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Vitamin K given to newborn — mother asks why — nurse explains?
choices: B. To prevent bleeding — baby's gut is sterile and cannot make vitamin K
questions.rationale: Newborns lack intestinal flora needed to synthesize vitamin K; prophylactic injection prevents hemorrhagic disease.
choice_rationales: ✘ Vitamin K does not have antimicrobial properties.
✔ The sterile neonatal gut cannot synthesize vitamin K; without it, clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X are inactive, causing hemorrhagic disease.
✘ Erythromycin ointment is given for eye prophylaxis (ophthalmia neonatorum), not vitamin K.
✘ Jaundice results from bilirubin accumulation; phototherapy treats jaundice, not vitamin K.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Methergine assessment BEFORE administration?
questions.rationale: Methergine is a potent vasoconstrictor; hypertension is an absolute contraindication.
choice_rationales: ✘ Asthma history is a relative contraindication for some utertonics but blood pressure is the critical check for Methergine.
✔ Methergine causes profound vasoconstriction; giving it to a hypertensive patient can cause stroke or fatal BP spike.
✘ Capillary refill assesses peripheral perfusion but is not the priority pre-Methergine assessment.
✘ DVT assessment is important in the postpartum period but is not specific to Methergine administration.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Early sign of compensatory hypovolemic shock?
questions.rationale: Tachycardia is the FIRST compensatory response to reduced circulating volume.
choice_rationales: ✘ Oliguria is an early sign but tachycardia precedes it as the earliest compensation.
✘ Cold clammy skin occurs as vasoconstriction intensifies — a sign of progressive shock.
✔ The sympathetic nervous system immediately increases heart rate to maintain cardiac output when volume drops.
✘ Narrowing pulse pressure is a later cardiovascular sign of shock as systolic pressure falls.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: BUBBLE-HE — the 'L' stands for?
questions.rationale: Lochia assessment (color, amount, odor) is an essential component of postpartum evaluation.
choice_rationales: ✘ Lacerations are assessed under 'E' (episiotomy/lacerations) in the BUBBLE-HE framework.
✔ L = Lochia; assessing postpartum vaginal discharge for characteristics helps detect infection or hemorrhage.
✘ Lactation is assessed under 'B' (Breasts) in the framework.
✘ Labor has already concluded in the postpartum period; it is not part of BUBBLE-HE.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Umbilical cord prolapse — client position?
questions.rationale: These positions use gravity to shift the fetus off the prolapsed cord, maintaining oxygen flow.
choice_rationales: ✘ High Fowler's increases gravity's effect on the fetal presenting part, worsening cord compression.
✘ Supine allows continued cord compression by the presenting part.
✔ Knee-chest or Trendelenburg positions use gravity to move the fetus away from the cord until emergency C-section.
✘ Lithotomy is used for delivery, not for relieving cord compression.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Placenta Previa client sent home on bed rest — statement indicating need for further teaching?
choices: A. 'I'll call the doctor if I have any bleeding even without pain.'
B. 'My partner and I can continue sexual intercourse carefully.'
C. 'I'll stay on bed rest and only get up to use the bathroom.'
D. 'I should avoid heavy lifting or strenuous exercise.'
questions.rationale: Pelvic rest (nothing inserted vaginally) is mandatory in placenta previa; intercourse can cause catastrophic hemorrhage.
choice_rationales: ✘ Reporting painless bleeding is correct; placenta previa causes painless bright red bleeding.
✔ Sexual intercourse is absolutely contraindicated in placenta previa; vaginal penetration near the placenta risks massive hemorrhage.
✘ Strict bed rest with bathroom privileges is appropriate home management.
✘ Avoiding lifting and strenuous activity prevents abdominal pressure that could trigger bleeding.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Negative fetal fibronectin test at 34 weeks — most accurate interpretation?
choices: A. Will definitely deliver within 48 hours
B. Increased risk of amniotic infection
C. 95–99% probability of NOT delivering in next 2 weeks
D. Fetus has reached lung maturity
questions.rationale: A negative fFN has high negative predictive value; preterm birth in the next 2 weeks is very unlikely.
choice_rationales: ✘ A negative fFN actually means delivery is UNLIKELY within the next 2 weeks.
✘ fFN is not an infection marker; it tests for a glycoprotein that appears when membranes begin separating.
✔ The test's strength is its negative predictive value; a negative result reliably predicts that preterm birth will not occur soon.
✘ fFN does not assess fetal lung maturity; lecithin/sphingomyelin ratio or lamellar body count does.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 38-week client with sudden tearing abdominal pain, referred shoulder pain, fetal parts difficult to palpate, absent FHR — priority?
choices: A. Vaginal exam for cord prolapse
questions.rationale: Classic uterine rupture signs require immediate surgical intervention to save mother and fetus.
choice_rationales: ✘ Vaginal exam is contraindicated and wastes critical time in uterine rupture.
✔ Uterine rupture with absent FHR is a surgical emergency; immediate laparotomy is the only intervention.
✘ Oxytocin in uterine rupture is absolutely contraindicated; it would worsen the catastrophic event.
✘ High Fowler's does not address the hemorrhagic emergency of uterine rupture.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Gestational diabetes in second stage — fetal head emerges then retracts (turtle sign) — first manual intervention?
questions.rationale: McRoberts flattens the lumbar lordosis and rotates the symphysis, increasing the functional pelvic outlet.
choice_rationales: ✘ Fundal pressure (Kristeller) is strictly CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia; it worsens impaction.
✔ McRoberts maneuver is the first-line intervention for shoulder dystocia — flex maternal thighs sharply against abdomen.
✘ Vacuum is not used during shoulder dystocia; it can cause cervical injury without resolving the impaction.
✘ C-section is considered only after all vaginal maneuvers have failed in shoulder dystocia.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: DIC confirmation after emergency C-section for abruption — expected lab result?
questions.rationale: DIC is confirmed by elevated D-dimer and fibrin degradation products from pathological fibrinolysis.
choice_rationales: ✘ Fibrinogen is CONSUMED in DIC due to excessive clotting; levels fall, not rise.
✔ D-dimer and FDPs are byproducts of fibrin breakdown; their elevation confirms the fibrinolytic storm of DIC.
✘ PT is prolonged (not shortened) in DIC because clotting factors are depleted.
✘ Hemoglobin is DECREASED in DIC from hemorrhage and hemolysis.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Oxytocin induction with 6 contractions in 10 minutes (tachysystole) — first action?
questions.rationale: Removing the cause of tachysystole (oxytocin) is the primary intervention to restore normal contraction frequency.
choice_rationales: ✔ Stopping oxytocin immediately reduces uterine hyperstimulation, restoring adequate placental blood flow between contractions.
✘ IV fluids are a secondary supportive measure after stopping the medication.
✘ Oxygen may be given as a supportive measure but removing the cause is the priority.
✘ Checking dilation does not address the tachysystole emergency.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Android pelvis noted during labor assessment — anticipated complication?
choices: B. Difficult labor with high C-section risk
questions.rationale: Android (heart-shaped, male-type) pelvis has narrow dimensions causing CPD and operative delivery.
choice_rationales: ✘ Precipitous delivery occurs with a gynecoid pelvis and favorable fetal presentation.
✔ Android pelvic architecture causes cephalopelvic disproportion, labor dystocia, and increased C-section rate.
✘ Spontaneous rotation to occiput anterior is favored by a gynecoid pelvis, not android.
✘ Amniotic fluid volume is not related to pelvic architecture.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Lochia alba — what color?
questions.rationale: Lochia alba (days 10 to 6 weeks) is white or yellowish, consisting of leukocytes, decidual cells, and mucus.
choice_rationales: ✘ Dark red describes lochia rubra (days 1–3).
✘ Pinkish-brown describes lochia serosa (days 4–10).
✔ Lochia alba is the final postpartum discharge stage — creamy white/yellow from leukocytes and cellular debris.
✘ Green discharge suggests infection and is never a normal lochia color.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: NST shows only one FHR acceleration in 40 minutes — result classification?
questions.rationale: A reactive NST requires ≥2 accelerations meeting criteria in 20 minutes; one acceleration is non-reactive.
choice_rationales: ✘ Reactive requires at least 2 qualifying accelerations (≥15 bpm × ≥15 seconds) in 20 minutes.
✔ Failure to meet the reactive criteria is classified as non-reactive, requiring further evaluation (BPP or contraction stress test).
✘ Equivocal (suspicious) is a BPP classification, not the primary NST term.
✘ 'Satisfactory' is not standard NST terminology.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Client at 41 weeks during oxytocin induction — nurse MOST vigilant about which sign?
questions.rationale: Uterine tetany (sustained contraction >90 seconds) from oxytocin reduces fetal oxygenation.
choice_rationales: ✔ Uterine tetany (tachysystole) caused by oxytocin overdose compromises uteroplacental blood flow, risking fetal hypoxia and uterine rupture.
✘ Pain management is important but does not represent an oxytocin-specific safety concern.
✘ Cord prolapse is a risk with SROM and presenting part not engaged, not specific to oxytocin induction.
✘ Hypoglycemia is not related to oxytocin administration.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse identifies uterine rupture — priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Uterine rupture causes catastrophic hemorrhage; preventing hypovolemic shock is the immediate priority.
choice_rationales: ✘ Catheterization is part of preparation for surgery but not the priority intervention.
✘ Bed rest does not address the hemorrhagic emergency of uterine rupture.
✘ Blood specimens are needed for cross-matching but obtaining them is secondary to treating shock.
✔ Hypovolemic shock from massive hemorrhage is the life-threatening emergency; fluid resuscitation, oxygen, and preparation for emergency surgery are immediate priorities.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: NST — nurse instructs client to press control button?
choices: A. After every three fetal movements
B. At the start of each contraction
C. At end of each fetal movement
D. At the START of each fetal movement
questions.rationale: Marking the start of fetal movement allows correlation with subsequent FHR acceleration on the tracing.
choice_rationales: ✘ Marking after multiple movements does not allow precise correlation with the FHR tracing.
✘ NST is not a contraction-based test; contractions are not the relevant trigger.
✘ Marking at the end misses the correlation between movement onset and FHR acceleration.
✔ The client marks each movement at its onset so the nurse can verify that FHR accelerates in response.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: BPP score of 8 at 30 weeks — interpretation?
choices: C. Fetus not in distress at this time
questions.rationale: BPP score of 8/10 is reassuring and indicates adequate fetal oxygenation and neurobehavioral function.
choice_rationales: ✘ Delivery within 24 hours is indicated for BPP ≤4 with abnormal AFI, not 8/10.
✘ Repeat in 24 hours is for BPP = 6 (equivocal result).
✔ A BPP of 8/10 (or 8/8 if AFI not scored) indicates good fetal status; routine follow-up per gestational age.
✘ Weekly repeat may be indicated for high-risk pregnancies but a score of 8 does not require urgent repeat.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: First question to ask client arriving stating she is in labor?
choices: A. Who is your support person?
B. Do you have chronic conditions?
D. When is your estimated due date?
questions.rationale: Gestational age is the most critical initial data point determining management (term vs preterm).
choice_rationales: ✘ Support persons are noted but do not guide immediate clinical management.
✘ Chronic conditions are important history but secondary to establishing gestational age.
✘ Allergy assessment is important but not the first question in labor triage.
✔ The EDD establishes gestational age — the foundation for all subsequent management decisions.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Previous reduction mammoplasty and wants to breastfeed — nurse provides?
choices: A. Consult surgeon to see if breastfeeding possible
B. Should be able to breastfeed without issues
C. Support decision but may need to supplement with formula
D. Breastfeeding not recommended after this surgery
questions.rationale: Reduction mammoplasty may reduce milk supply depending on technique and glandular tissue removed.
choice_rationales: ✘ Consulting the surgeon may be helpful but the nurse can already provide accurate information.
✘ Reduction mammoplasty can impair milk supply depending on the degree of glandular tissue removal.
✔ Many women breastfeed successfully after reduction, but supply may be insufficient; formula supplementation may be needed.
✘ Breastfeeding after mammoplasty is not contraindicated; it may simply require supplementation.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Multigravida in early labor asks for snack — best choice?
questions.rationale: Light, easily digestible protein and carbohydrates are appropriate in early labor.
choice_rationales: ✘ Soup contains adequate electrolytes but high sodium; yogurt is lighter and more balanced.
✘ Peanut butter cookies are high fat and slow to digest, increasing aspiration risk if emergency anesthesia is needed.
✘ Cereal with milk is bulky and higher in fat; yogurt is more appropriate for early labor.
✔ Yogurt is light, well-tolerated, provides protein and carbohydrates, and has low aspiration risk if emergency surgery is required.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Rhythm/BBT method — unsafe period for sexual activity?
choices: A. When BBT returns to preovulatory levels
B. After 3 consecutive days elevated BBT with clear thin cervical mucus
C. When BBT rises 0.1–0.2° on days 2–3
D. During breast tenderness and Mittelschmerz
questions.rationale: Three consecutive elevated BBT days with fertile mucus confirms the post-ovulatory safe period has begun.
choice_rationales: ✘ Return to preovulatory BBT does not confirm post-ovulatory safety; three elevated days are needed.
✔ Three elevated BBT days confirm ovulation has occurred; combined with clear thin mucus, this identifies the post-ovulatory infertile phase.
✘ A slight rise on days 2–3 of the cycle occurs at menses; the post-ovulatory rise is the relevant marker.
✘ Breast tenderness and Mittelschmerz may indicate ovulation is occurring — this is a FERTILE period, not a safe one.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Second stage labor — primary purpose of frequent contraction monitoring?
choices: C. Determine if additional fluids needed
D. Ensure contractions facilitate fetal descent
questions.rationale: Adequate contraction strength and frequency during the second stage are essential for effective fetal descent.
choice_rationales: ✘ Position is determined by Leopold's maneuvers and vaginal exam, not contraction monitoring.
✘ Pain assessment is separate from contraction monitoring for descent purposes.
✘ Fluid needs are assessed separately; contraction monitoring focuses on labor progress.
✔ The second stage depends on effective uterine contractions to create the expulsive force needed for fetal descent.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Eclampsia client seizes — priority?
questions.rationale: Airway patency ensures oxygenation during and after the seizure — the immediate life-saving priority.
choice_rationales: ✘ Left lateral tilt is important to prevent aortocaval compression but airway takes priority during a seizure.
✘ Tongue blades are no longer recommended during seizures; they can cause oral injury.
✘ Padded rails protect from injury but are secondary to maintaining the airway.
✔ During eclamptic seizure, maintaining an open airway prevents hypoxia — the most immediate life threat.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Variable decelerations on fetal monitor — first action?
questions.rationale: Variable decelerations from cord compression are often corrected by repositioning to relieve pressure on the cord.
choice_rationales: ✘ Placenta previa causes late decelerations, not variable decelerations.
✘ C-section is considered only if position change and other measures fail to resolve the decelerations.
✘ Oxygen may be given secondarily but position change addresses the cause (cord compression) directly.
✔ Repositioning (lateral, knee-chest, or Trendelenburg) relieves cord compression and resolves most variable decelerations.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Breastfeeding assessment — which finding is INAPPROPRIATE (should not be present)?
questions.rationale: Lip smacking indicates a poor, shallow latch; proper attachment should not produce smacking sounds.
choice_rationales: ✘ Assessing maternal comfort and positioning is an appropriate and important breastfeeding evaluation.
✘ Audible swallowing confirms the baby is actively transferring milk — a positive finding.
✔ Smacking lips indicate the baby is not properly latched; a good latch produces a deep seal with no smacking sounds.
✘ Deep attachment covering most of the areola is the goal of effective breastfeeding.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Third trimester — symptom to report immediately?
questions.rationale: Blurred vision in the third trimester is a warning sign of severe preeclampsia and possible eclampsia.
choice_rationales: ✔ Blurred vision, headache, and epigastric pain in the third trimester indicate severe preeclampsia requiring immediate evaluation.
✘ Hemorrhoids are common and uncomfortable but not dangerous; local measures are recommended.
✘ Increased vaginal mucus (leukorrhea) is a normal pregnancy finding.
✘ Mild dyspnea on exertion is common due to diaphragm elevation; sudden severe dyspnea requires reporting.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Post-delivery: nurse uses both hands palpating fundus — purpose?
questions.rationale: Supporting the lower uterine segment prevents the fundus from being displaced inferiorly (inversion).
choice_rationales: ✘ Recovery speed is not the purpose of bimanual fundal assessment.
✘ Massage promotes contraction but the bimanual technique specifically guards against inversion.
✔ Stabilizing the lower uterine segment with one hand while the other massages prevents the dangerous complication of uterine inversion.
✘ Size estimation is a component of fundal assessment but not the reason for bimanual technique.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Multigravida history of rapid labor exclaims 'baby is coming!' — first action?
choices: B. Listen to fetal heart rate
questions.rationale: Crowning confirms imminent delivery and directs all subsequent emergency actions.
choice_rationales: ✔ Visualizing the perineum for crowning immediately determines if birth is seconds away, guiding urgent decisions.
✘ Auscultating FHR is important but secondary to confirming whether delivery is imminent.
✘ Notifying the birth attendant is necessary but the nurse must first assess for crowning.
✘ Contraction timing is less urgent than confirming whether the baby's head is visible.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Blastocyst travel time from fertilization to uterine implantation?
questions.rationale: The blastocyst takes approximately 7 days to travel from the fallopian tube to the uterine cavity for implantation.
choice_rationales: ✔ After fertilization in the ampulla of the fallopian tube, the blastocyst takes approximately 7 days to reach and implant in the uterus.
✘ 2 days is insufficient; the zygote is still in early cleavage stages in the fallopian tube at this point.
✘ 14 weeks is a gestational age milestone, not the implantation timeline.
✘ 10 days is slightly longer than the typical implantation window of 6–10 days, with day 7 being most common.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 40-week client with contractions every 2 minutes, fetal station +4 — priority?
choices: C. Check if membranes have ruptured
D. Place in bed for fetal monitoring
questions.rationale: Station +4 means the presenting part is 4 cm below the ischial spines — delivery is imminent.
choice_rationales: ✔ Station +4 indicates the fetal head is nearly at the perineum; birth preparation takes absolute priority.
✘ Comfort measures are secondary when birth is seconds to minutes away.
✘ Membrane status is important but secondary to immediate birth preparation.
✘ Placing the client in bed when birth is imminent delays necessary preparations.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Prenatal history — which finding suggests a GENETIC risk factor?
choices: C. Child diagnosed with cystic fibrosis
D. Prior preterm labor at 32 weeks
questions.rationale: CF is autosomal recessive; having an affected child confirms both parents carry the mutation.
choice_rationales: ✘ Age 25 is within optimal reproductive age range with no increased genetic risk.
✘ Rubella is a teratogenic environmental exposure, not a genetic risk factor.
✔ Cystic fibrosis is autosomal recessive; an affected child means both parents are carriers, making each subsequent pregnancy have a 25% chance of CF.
✘ Prior preterm labor is an obstetric risk factor, not a genetic one.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Fourth-degree laceration — which action is CONTRAINDICATED?
choices: A. Cold packs for first 12–24 hours
C. Using two or more peripads for cushioning
questions.rationale: Stacking peripads does not provide therapeutic support and may cause additional perineal pressure.
choice_rationales: ✘ Ice application for the first 12–24 hours reduces edema and pain — appropriate care.
✘ Kegel exercises promote perineal muscle healing and are appropriate after fourth-degree laceration.
✔ Using multiple peripads does not provide support or healing benefit and may cause discomfort; a single pad for absorption is appropriate.
✘ Sitz baths promote hygiene and comfort and are prescribed for perineal wound care.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 36-week prenatal visit — question BEST assessing readiness for parenthood?
choices: A. Have you attended prenatal classes?
B. What adjustments have you made at home for the baby?
C. Describe your usual daily meals?
D. Are you considering epidural anesthesia?
questions.rationale: Home preparation provides insight into both practical readiness and psychological adjustment to parenthood.
choice_rationales: ✘ Prenatal class attendance indicates education-seeking but not actual readiness or home preparation.
✔ Asking about home adjustments reveals whether the family has made practical and psychological preparations.
✘ Dietary assessment is a routine prenatal check, not a readiness indicator.
✘ Pain management preference does not reflect readiness for the parenting role.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Postpartum rubella vaccine teaching — include?
choices: A. Avoid pregnancy for 3 months after vaccination
B. Vaccine prevents future fetal congenital anomalies
C. Avoid contact with children who have rubella
questions.rationale: Rubella vaccine is live-attenuated; pregnancy within 3 months post-vaccination risks fetal harm.
choice_rationales: ✔ The live rubella vaccine is teratogenic; contraception must be used for at least 3 months post-administration.
✘ The vaccine protects the mother from future infection; it does not directly protect the fetus.
✘ The vaccinated person does not need to avoid rubella-infected contacts; the vaccine builds immunity.
✘ Rubella is '3-day measles'; 7-day measles is rubeola — a different disease.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 18–40 weeks — procedure to detect fetal anomalies?
questions.rationale: Ultrasound is the primary non-invasive screening tool throughout pregnancy for anatomical abnormalities.
choice_rationales: ✘ Fetoscopy is rarely used and carries significant complication risks; ultrasound has replaced it for routine anomaly screening.
✘ Amniocentesis is performed at 15–20 weeks primarily for chromosomal analysis, not routine anatomical screening.
✘ CVS is performed at 10–13 weeks for early chromosomal testing, not for 18–40 week anatomical surveys.
✔ The anatomy scan (detailed ultrasound) at 18–20 weeks is the primary tool for detecting structural fetal anomalies.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Primigravida at 35 weeks in active labor, no prenatal care, admits cocaine use — nurse notifies FIRST?
choices: A. Physician who will attend the delivery
B. Chaplain in case fetus dies
D. Nursing unit manager to notify agencies
questions.rationale: The immediate clinical emergency (preterm labor, cocaine exposure) requires immediate physician involvement.
choice_rationales: ✔ The attending physician must be notified immediately to manage the preterm labor, cocaine-related complications, and plan for a high-risk delivery.
✘ Chaplain notification is premature; the clinical team must be assembled first.
✘ Security is not involved unless a safety threat exists; this is a medical emergency.
✘ The unit manager may be notified but the physician providing immediate care takes priority.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: After vaginal exam: ROA position, -1 station — fetal presenting part is?
choices: C. At the level of ischial spines
questions.rationale: Negative station numbers indicate the presenting part is above the ischial spines by that many centimeters.
choice_rationales: ✘ 1 cm below the spines would be +1 station.
✘ Station is always described relative to the ischial spines.
✘ At the ischial spines is station 0 (engagement).
✔ Station -1 means the presenting part is 1 cm above the level of the ischial spines, not yet engaged.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 14-week client: uterine size 18–20 weeks, GTD suspected — ultrasound expected finding?
questions.rationale: Complete molar pregnancy shows the classic 'snowstorm' appearance of grapelike vesicles on ultrasound.
choice_rationales: ✘ In complete molar pregnancy, there is no fetal tissue; a malformed fetus would suggest a partial mole.
✔ Molar pregnancy (complete) appears as echogenic grapelike or snowstorm clusters on ultrasound — abnormal trophoblastic proliferation.
✘ An empty gestational sac (blighted ovum) does not produce the excessively large uterus or elevated hCG of a molar pregnancy.
✘ Ectopic pregnancy is extrauterine; molar pregnancy is within the uterus.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Vaginal delivery with midline episiotomy — priority nursing diagnosis?
choices: B. Risk for deficient fluid volume related to hemorrhage
C. Risk for infection related to delivery
D. Urinary retention related to periurethral edema
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is the leading cause of maternal mortality; hemorrhage risk takes priority.
choice_rationales: ✘ Pain is important but not life-threatening; hemorrhage is the priority.
✔ Postpartum hemorrhage risk is the highest priority in the immediate postpartum period given its mortality implications.
✘ Infection risk is real but develops over days; hemorrhage can be fatal within minutes.
✘ Urinary retention is uncomfortable but not life-threatening compared to hemorrhage.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Client states blurred/double vision should be reported immediately — this indicates?
choices: B. Unnecessary concern — fatigue is common
C. Needs further teaching — only double vision is serious
D. Should only report if accompanied by headache
questions.rationale: Visual disturbances are danger signs of preeclampsia and must be reported immediately.
choice_rationales: ✔ Blurred or double vision in pregnancy are cerebral signs of severe preeclampsia and require immediate evaluation.
✘ Visual changes are danger signs, not normal fatigue symptoms.
✘ Both blurred AND double vision are serious; the client should report either.
✘ Visual changes alone (without headache) still require immediate reporting.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 39-week client bleeds after cocaine use — most likely complication?
questions.rationale: Cocaine causes acute vasoconstriction reducing placental perfusion and triggering placental abruption.
choice_rationales: ✔ Cocaine-induced vasospasm in uterine vessels reduces blood flow and can shear the placenta from the uterine wall.
✘ Ectopic pregnancy presents at less than 12 weeks; at 39 weeks a term intrauterine pregnancy is present.
✘ Spontaneous abortion occurs before 20 weeks; at 39 weeks this is not applicable.
✘ Placenta previa causes painless bleeding; cocaine causes acute abruption with painful bleeding.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Client understanding of episiotomy purpose?
choices: B. Shortens the second stage of labor
questions.rationale: Episiotomy enlarges the vaginal opening to speed crowning and delivery, shortening the second stage.
choice_rationales: ✘ Episiotomy does not affect third stage (placenta delivery) management.
✔ A controlled surgical incision of the perineum increases the vaginal outlet, shortening the second stage.
✘ The pelvic inlet is a bony structure that cannot be enlarged by an episiotomy.
✘ Episiotomy does not prevent edema; it reduces tearing but introduces its own wound healing needs.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Multigravida Type 1 diabetic at 27 weeks — fetal well-being monitoring advice?
choices: A. Limit physical activity to avoid straining fetus
B. Count fetal movements daily and report any decrease
C. Monitor blood glucose mornings only
D. Come weekly for FHR monitoring
questions.rationale: Kick counts are a simple, daily, at-home method for detecting early fetal compromise.
choice_rationales: ✘ Moderate physical activity is generally safe and beneficial in pregnancy; restriction is not routine advice.
✔ Daily fetal movement counting is the recommended first-line monitoring for fetal well-being in diabetic pregnancies.
✘ Blood glucose must be monitored multiple times daily in Type 1 diabetes, not just mornings.
✘ Weekly clinic visits for FHR monitoring are arranged by the provider; daily kick counts are the home monitoring advice.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Postpartum progressive physiological change?
questions.rationale: Lactation progressively develops postpartum, beginning with colostrum and transitioning to mature milk.
choice_rationales: ✘ Lochia is a retrogressive change — the body eliminating excess tissue from pregnancy.
✔ Lactation is a progressive change — the body builds capacity to nourish the newborn.
✘ Diuresis is a retrogressive change — the body excreting excess fluid accumulated during pregnancy.
✘ Uterine involution is retrogressive — the uterus returning to its pre-pregnancy size.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Contraction frequency rule of thumb for active labor?
questions.rationale: Active labor contractions every 2–3 minutes lasting 60–90 seconds are effective for cervical dilation.
choice_rationales: ✘ Contractions every 10–15 minutes describe early/latent labor, not active labor.
✔ Active labor contractions of 2–3 minute intervals and 60–90 second duration provide adequate uterine work without hyperstimulation.
✘ Contractions every minute lasting 2 minutes indicate tachysystole — a dangerous pattern.
✘ Hourly contractions are inadequate for active labor progression.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Postpartum client with vulvar edema, firm fundus, severe pain and rectal pressure — first action?
choices: C. Inspect perineum and vaginal area
questions.rationale: Intense rectal pressure with a firm fundus suggests a hematoma — assessment (inspection) must come first.
choice_rationales: ✘ Analgesics address pain but not the cause; assessment must precede intervention.
✘ Ice may be therapeutic for a hematoma but inspection comes first to confirm the diagnosis.
✔ The first step in any clinical concern is assessment; the nurse must visualize the perineum to identify a hematoma.
✘ Pressure dressings may be used after diagnosis but applying them without assessment is inappropriate.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Physiological cause of Supine Hypotensive Syndrome in third trimester?
choices: C. Heavy uterus compressing inferior vena cava
questions.rationale: The gravid uterus compresses the IVC when supine, reducing venous return and causing hypotension.
choice_rationales: ✘ Increased blood volume is protective against hypotension; it does not cause it.
✘ Hormonal relaxation of vessel walls contributes to general hypotension in pregnancy but not the positional syndrome.
✔ The heavy uterus (at term ~1000g plus contents) compresses the inferior vena cava in the supine position, dramatically reducing cardiac preload.
✘ Anemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity but does not cause position-specific hypotension.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: BPP score 4/10 at 34 weeks — suggests?
questions.rationale: BPP scores of 4 or below indicate fetal compromise, often requiring further evaluation or delivery.
choice_rationales: ✘ Normal BPP is 8–10; 4/10 is an abnormal result.
✔ A BPP score of 4 suggests compromised fetal oxygenation; further evaluation (CST, delivery consideration) is indicated.
✘ BPP does not diagnose gestational diabetes; GTT is used for that.
✘ Sleep cycles can reduce BPP score transiently, but a score of 4 requires evaluation to rule out genuine compromise.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: 4 T's of PPH — boggy uterus corresponds to which 'T'?
questions.rationale: Uterine atony (loss of muscle tone) is the MOST common cause of postpartum hemorrhage.
choice_rationales: ✔ Tone = Atony; 80% of PPH cases result from failure of the uterus to contract after delivery.
✘ Trauma includes lacerations, episiotomy, and uterine rupture — the uterus is injured, not atonic.
✘ Tissue refers to retained placental fragments preventing uterine contraction.
✘ Thrombin refers to coagulopathies (DIC, thrombocytopenia) impairing clot formation.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Preterm labor Nifedipine therapy — finding requiring immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Severe hypotension from nifedipine reduces uteroplacental blood flow, endangering the fetus.
choice_rationales: ✘ HR 98 bpm is a mild tachycardia, an expected vasodilation effect of nifedipine.
✘ Mild headache is a common side effect of nifedipine and does not require immediate intervention.
✔ Severe hypotension (BP 82/50) impairs uterine blood flow; fetal hypoxia may result, requiring immediate treatment.
✘ Uterine relaxation between contractions is the desired therapeutic effect of tocolysis.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: During bearing down — nurse coaches client to push?
choices: A. Between contractions to prevent rupture
B. When she feels rectal pressure
C. Anytime she feels like bearing down
questions.rationale: Pushing with contractions coordinates maternal effort with uterine force for maximum effectiveness.
choice_rationales: ✘ Pushing between contractions wastes energy and provides no uterine assistance.
✘ Rectal pressure is a cue to push, but the nurse should coach pushing with contractions for maximum benefit.
✘ Pushing at random times without contraction coordination is inefficient.
✔ Bearing down during contractions combines maternal pushing force with uterine contractile power, accelerating fetal descent.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Labor induction monitoring — most dangerous sign to watch for?
questions.rationale: Uterine tetany (sustained contraction >90 seconds) causes fetal asphyxia and may lead to uterine rupture.
choice_rationales: ✔ Tachysystole/tetany from oxytocin hyperstimulation reduces placental oxygenation between contractions, causing fetal distress.
✘ Severe pain is a subjective symptom that requires assessment; it is not the most dangerous objective sign.
✘ Cord prolapse is a risk with AROM in unengaged presentations, not specific to induction monitoring.
✘ Hypoglycemia is unrelated to oxytocin induction monitoring.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Following precipitous delivery with fourth-degree laceration — which is CONTRAINDICATED?
choices: C. Using two or more peripads for cushioning
questions.rationale: Doubling peripads does not provide support and may increase perineal pressure and discomfort.
choice_rationales: ✘ Ice packs are appropriate for reducing edema and pain in the first 24 hours.
✘ Kegel exercises begin after pain decreases and promote perineal healing.
✔ Two or more peripads are contraindicated; they do not provide therapeutic benefit and may cause unwanted pressure on the wound.
✘ Prescribed sitz baths provide hygiene and comfort and are appropriate post-laceration care.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Labor client history of precipitous labor — nurse Lena's FIRST action when client says baby is coming?
questions.rationale: In imminent delivery, confirming crowning determines if birth is seconds away.
choice_rationales: ✔ Visual confirmation of crowning is the first assessment that determines whether delivery is immediately happening.
✘ FHR assessment is important but secondary to confirming whether birth is imminent.
✘ Notifying the attendant happens after assessing whether the birth is truly imminent.
✘ Contraction timing is less urgent than confirming the birth is happening.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Postpartum hemorrhage client: which is EARLIEST sign of compensatory shock?
questions.rationale: Tachycardia is the first compensatory mechanism activated by the sympathetic nervous system in hemorrhagic shock.
choice_rationales: ✘ Oliguria is an early sign but tachycardia precedes it as the initial sympathetic response.
✘ Cold clammy skin represents progressive vasoconstriction — a sign of worsening, not early shock.
✔ Increased heart rate is the first compensation the body makes to maintain cardiac output when blood volume decreases.
✘ Narrowing pulse pressure is a later sign as systolic pressure begins to fall in uncompensated shock.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Client's last prenatal visit — which statement shows CORRECT understanding of danger signs?
choices: A. Report blurred or double vision immediately
B. Report if feeling tired after resting
questions.rationale: Visual disturbances are classic warning signs of preeclampsia requiring emergency evaluation.
choice_rationales: ✔ Blurred or double vision in the third trimester is a danger sign of preeclampsia — an obstetric emergency.
✘ Fatigue is a normal pregnancy complaint and does not require immediate reporting.
✘ Urinary frequency is normal in pregnancy due to uterine pressure on the bladder.
✘ Nausea is common in early pregnancy; in the third trimester, severe nausea with vomiting warrants concern but nausea alone is not a danger sign.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Rh isoimmunization — nurse explains sensitization occurs when?
choices: A. Rh+ mother carries Rh- fetus
B. Rh- mother carries Rh+ fetus
C. Rh+ mother carries Rh+ fetus
D. Rh- mother carries Rh- fetus
questions.rationale: Rh+ fetal cells entering the Rh- maternal circulation trigger anti-D antibody production.
choice_rationales: ✘ An Rh+ mother cannot be sensitized because she already has the Rh antigen; no immune response is triggered.
✔ The Rh- mother's immune system recognizes fetal Rh+ RBCs as foreign, producing antibodies that can harm future Rh+ pregnancies.
✘ Both Rh+ — no incompatibility, no sensitization.
✘ Both Rh- — no Rh antigen present on either side; no sensitization possible.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Sarah — 10 weeks, open cervical os, bright red bleeding — type of abortion?
questions.rationale: Open cervical os with bleeding confirms the abortion cannot be prevented — inevitable.
choice_rationales: ✔ An inevitable abortion has an open cervix with bleeding; the pregnancy cannot continue.
✘ Incomplete abortion means some but not all products of conception have passed.
✘ Threatened abortion has a CLOSED cervix with bleeding; the pregnancy may continue.
✘ Missed abortion is fetal death with a closed cervix and absent bleeding.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Second stage labor — uterine contraction monitoring PRIMARY purpose?
choices: D. Ensure contractions facilitate fetal descent
questions.rationale: Strong, well-coordinated second-stage contractions are the primary force driving fetal descent.
choice_rationales: ✘ Head position is determined by clinical assessment, not contraction monitoring.
✘ Pain assessment is separate from monitoring contraction effectiveness.
✘ Fluid management is separate from second-stage contraction monitoring.
✔ Contraction adequacy directly determines the effectiveness of the expulsive forces in the second stage.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: First-time mother post-delivery — nurse carefully uses both hands on fundus to?
questions.rationale: Bimanual fundal assessment requires one hand below to prevent inversion while the other massages.
choice_rationales: ✘ Recovery speed is not the purpose of this assessment technique.
✘ While massage does promote contraction, the bimanual technique's specific purpose is inversion prevention.
✔ Supporting the lower uterine segment prevents the fundus from being pushed through the cervix during palpation.
✘ Size measurement is done clinically but is not why two hands are used.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Type 1 DM at 27 weeks — BEST fetal monitoring advice?
choices: B. Daily fetal movement counting, report decrease
questions.rationale: Daily kick counting is the primary at-home fetal surveillance tool for high-risk pregnancies.
choice_rationales: ✘ Moderate activity is generally recommended; unnecessary restriction is not evidence-based.
✔ Decreased fetal movement is an early warning sign of fetal compromise; daily kick counts allow rapid detection.
✘ Type 1 diabetes requires multiple daily glucose checks, not once per day.
✘ Clinical FHR monitoring is arranged by the provider but is not the daily home monitoring advice.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse monitoring induction — which sign is MOST dangerous to watch for?
questions.rationale: Tetany eliminates the relaxation phase needed for placental reperfusion between contractions.
choice_rationales: ✔ Uterine tetany causes sustained hypoxia without relief between contractions, potentially leading to fetal death and uterine rupture.
✘ Pain is managed with analgesia and is subjective; tetany is an objective life-threatening sign.
✘ Cord prolapse is a PROM/SROM complication; not specific to induction monitoring.
✘ Hypoglycemia is a maternal metabolic concern unrelated to uterine activity monitoring.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse Green: uterine rupture occurs — priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Maternal hemorrhagic shock from uterine rupture is immediately life-threatening.
choice_rationales: ✘ Catheterization is part of surgical preparation but not the immediate priority.
✘ Bed rest does not address the hemorrhagic emergency.
✘ Blood specimens for cross-matching are important but resuscitation comes first.
✔ IV access, fluid resuscitation, oxygen, and preparation for emergency surgery are immediate interventions to combat hypovolemic shock.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Prenatal class: respiratory change expected during pregnancy?
questions.rationale: Progesterone drives increased tidal volume to meet the elevated metabolic oxygen demands of pregnancy.
choice_rationales: ✘ Tidal volume INCREASES in pregnancy; a decrease would impair fetal oxygenation.
✔ Progesterone acts as a respiratory stimulant, increasing tidal volume by approximately 40% during pregnancy.
✘ Oxygen consumption increases 20–40% in pregnancy to support fetal and maternal needs.
✘ The airways widen (bronchodilation) under progesterone influence; narrowing does not occur.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: GTPAL for client currently pregnant with one birth at 39 weeks (living) and one miscarriage at 10 weeks?
questions.rationale: G=3 (current + full term + miscarriage); T=1; P=0; A=1; L=1 — using complete GTPAL system.
choice_rationales: ✔ G counts all pregnancies (3); T=1 (39-week term birth); P=0 (no preterm births); A=1 (miscarriage); L=1 (living child).
✘ G=2 only counts 2 pregnancies, missing the current pregnancy.
✘ T=2 incorrectly counts the miscarriage as a term birth.
✘ G=2 underestimates gravida and P=1 incorrectly categorizes the 10-week miscarriage as a preterm birth.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse Taylor: monitor a client during induction — MOST vigilant for?
questions.rationale: Oxytocin can cause tachysystole leading to fetal distress and uterine rupture.
choice_rationales: ✔ Tachysystole (>5 contractions/10 min or tetany) reduces placental perfusion and is the most dangerous oxytocin complication.
✘ Pain management is important but is not the dangerous sign specific to induction monitoring.
✘ Cord prolapse relates to membrane rupture with floating presenting part, not specifically to induction.
✘ Hypoglycemia is a metabolic concern unrelated to oxytocin induction vigilance.

questions.subject: Maternal & Child Health Nursing
questions.stem: Station 0 in labor refers to presenting part at the level of?
questions.rationale: Station 0 = presenting part at the ischial spines = engaged.
choice_rationales: ✘ The pelvic inlet is the upper boundary of the true pelvis; the presenting part at the inlet is at station -5.
✔ Station 0 means the presenting part has reached the ischial spines — the reference point for all station measurements.
✘ The pelvic outlet is reached at approximately station +3 to +5.
✘ The perineum is visible at station +5 when crowning occurs.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: What is the most basic unit of society in community health nursing?
questions.rationale: The family is the basic unit of society and primary focus of CHN.
choice_rationales: ✘ The individual is the recipient of care but not the unit of focus in community health nursing.
✔ The family is the basic social unit; health behaviors, beliefs, and illness patterns are shaped within the family context.
✘ The community is the broader population but the family is the fundamental unit.
✘ The barangay is the smallest political unit but the family is the basic social unit.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Early detection and prompt treatment of disease is which prevention level?
questions.rationale: Secondary prevention targets early disease detection through screening and prompt treatment.
choice_rationales: ✘ Primary prevention prevents disease from occurring in the first place.
✔ Secondary prevention (e.g., screening programs) detects disease early before symptoms progress.
✘ Tertiary prevention focuses on rehabilitation and minimizing disability after disease is established.
✘ Quaternary prevention reduces over-medicalization; it is not standard epidemiological terminology.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Immunization is an example of which prevention level?
questions.rationale: Immunization prevents disease from occurring — the definition of primary prevention.
choice_rationales: ✔ Primary prevention eliminates risk before disease onset; vaccines create immunity before exposure.
✘ Secondary prevention detects existing disease; vaccines prevent disease occurrence.
✘ Tertiary prevention rehabilitates after disease; vaccines prevent the disease entirely.
✘ Quaternary prevention addresses over-diagnosis and over-treatment, not vaccine administration.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Philippine program providing free immunization to children under 2 years?
questions.rationale: EPI provides 10 free vaccines to all Filipino children under 2 years old.
choice_rationales: ✔ The Expanded Program on Immunization provides BCG, HepB, DPT, OPV, IPV, PCV, and measles vaccines free to children.
✘ Ligtas Tigdas was a measles eradication campaign, not the comprehensive immunization program.
✘ Garantisadong Pambata is a child health package providing health interventions to children under 6.
✘ PhilHealth is the national health insurance program, not an immunization program.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Number of NEW cases of a disease in a population during a specific time is called?
questions.rationale: Incidence measures new cases, reflecting disease risk in a population.
choice_rationales: ✘ Prevalence measures ALL existing cases (new and old) at a specific point or period.
✔ Incidence counts only new cases arising during a defined period, measuring how rapidly disease is occurring.
✘ Morbidity is a broader term for illness/disease burden; it does not specify new versus existing cases.
✘ Mortality measures deaths, not disease occurrence.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Proportion of deaths in a population over a specific period is the?
questions.rationale: Mortality rate quantifies deaths from all causes in a defined population and time period.
choice_rationales: ✘ Morbidity rate measures illness, not death.
✘ Incidence rate measures new disease cases, not deaths.
✔ Mortality rate = (deaths / population) × 1,000; it is the standard measure of death burden.
✘ Prevalence rate measures existing disease cases, not deaths.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Safe water supply is which type of disease prevention?
questions.rationale: Safe water prevents waterborne diseases before they occur — primary prevention.
choice_rationales: ✔ Providing safe, chlorinated water prevents exposure to pathogens before disease develops.
✘ Secondary prevention requires disease already exists to screen or treat; water safety is preventive.
✘ Tertiary prevention involves rehabilitation after disease; safe water prevents the disease.
✘ All prevention levels apply in public health; safe water clearly fits primary prevention.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: First step in the community health nursing process?
questions.rationale: Assessment (community diagnosis) provides the data foundation for all subsequent steps.
choice_rationales: ✘ Planning follows assessment; without data, planning is not evidence-based.
✘ Implementation follows planning; it cannot occur before assessment and planning.
✔ Assessment identifies community health needs, problems, and resources — the starting point.
✘ Evaluation is the final step measuring outcomes of implemented interventions.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Primary purpose of a home visit?
choices: B. Provide nursing care in the family's natural environment
D. Report family problems to authorities
questions.rationale: Home visits allow care delivery in the environment where the family lives and health behaviors occur.
choice_rationales: ✘ Financial assessment may be included but is not the primary purpose of a home visit.
✔ Home visits provide nursing care where families live, enabling assessment of home conditions that affect health.
✘ Compliance enforcement is authoritative; the nurse's role is educational and supportive.
✘ Reporting family problems is a specific legal obligation, not the primary purpose of home visits.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: BCG vaccine protects against which disease?
questions.rationale: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) provides protection against severe tuberculosis, especially TB meningitis.
choice_rationales: ✘ Measles vaccine (MMR) protects against measles; BCG does not.
✔ BCG provides 70–80% protection against severe forms of TB including meningitis and miliary TB in children.
✘ Hepatitis B vaccine series protects against HBV; BCG does not.
✘ DPT vaccine protects against diphtheria; BCG protects against tuberculosis.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: DPT vaccine protects against diphtheria, pertussis, and?
questions.rationale: DPT stands for Diphtheria, Pertussis (whooping cough), and Tetanus.
choice_rationales: ✘ Typhoid fever is prevented by typhoid vaccine; DPT does not cover typhoid.
✔ The three diseases in DPT are diphtheria (D), pertussis/whooping cough (P), and tetanus (T).
✘ Tuberculosis is prevented by BCG; DPT does not cover TB.
✘ Typhus is a rickettsial disease not covered by DPT.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: WHO recommended breastfeeding duration?
choices: B. Exclusive 6 months then continued to 2 years with complementary foods
questions.rationale: WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months with continued breastfeeding up to 2 years.
choice_rationales: ✘ Three months is insufficient; WHO recommends much longer exclusive breastfeeding.
✔ Exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months optimizes infant nutrition; continuation with complementary foods to 2 years maximizes benefits.
✘ One year underestimates the WHO recommendation of continued breastfeeding to age 2.
✘ Teeth eruption is not the WHO criterion for weaning.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Study of distribution and determinants of health events in populations?
questions.rationale: Epidemiology is defined as the study of disease distribution, determinants, and control in populations.
choice_rationales: ✘ Biostatistics applies statistical methods to biological and health data; it supports epidemiology but is not the same.
✔ Epidemiology examines who gets disease, where, when, and why — and how to prevent it.
✘ Community diagnosis is the assessment process in community health nursing, not a scientific discipline.
✘ Public health is the broader field encompassing many disciplines including epidemiology.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Cues to Action in the Health Belief Model refers to?
choices: C. Triggers that motivate health action
questions.rationale: Cues to Action are internal or external stimuli that prompt a person to take preventive health behavior.
choice_rationales: ✘ Perceived severity is the person's belief about how serious the disease is.
✘ Perceived susceptibility is the belief about personal risk of getting the disease.
✔ Cues to action (symptoms, media, reminder cards) are the triggers that bridge knowledge and behavior change.
✘ Perceived benefits are the positive outcomes expected from taking the health action.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: MOST appropriate teaching method for demonstrating wound care?
questions.rationale: Return demonstration verifies that the client can correctly perform the psychomotor skill.
choice_rationales: ✘ Lecture provides information but does not verify that the skill has been acquired.
✔ Return demonstration (teach-back for skills) allows the nurse to observe and correct technique before the client is independent.
✘ Group discussion is effective for attitudes and knowledge but not for skill verification.
✘ Pamphlets provide written information but cannot verify skill acquisition.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Barangay Health Workers are directly supervised by?
questions.rationale: BHWs work under the direct supervision of the Rural Health Midwife in their catchment area.
choice_rationales: ✘ The mayor is the political head of the barangay but not the direct clinical supervisor of BHWs.
✔ The Rural Health Midwife provides direct clinical supervision and guidance to Barangay Health Workers.
✘ The Regional Health Director oversees regional programs but is not the direct supervisor of BHWs.
✘ The Secretary of Health sets national policy but is not involved in direct BHW supervision.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Typhoid fever transmission route?
questions.rationale: Salmonella typhi is transmitted through contaminated food and water (fecal-oral route).
choice_rationales: ✘ Typhoid is not airborne; it requires ingestion of contaminated food or water.
✔ Fecal contamination of food and water by infected persons or carriers spreads Salmonella typhi.
✘ Typhoid is not vector-borne; it does not require an insect intermediary.
✘ Direct contact transmission is not the primary route for typhoid fever.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Primary vector for dengue fever?
questions.rationale: Aedes aegypti is the primary vector for dengue, Zika, chikungunya, and yellow fever.
choice_rationales: ✘ Anopheles mosquito is the vector for malaria, not dengue.
✘ Culex mosquito transmits Japanese encephalitis and filariasis, not dengue.
✔ Aedes aegypti breeds in clean stagnant water, bites during daytime, and transmits the dengue virus.
✘ Houseflies transmit gastrointestinal diseases mechanically but are not dengue vectors.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Most effective method preventing BOTH STIs and pregnancy?
questions.rationale: Condoms provide barrier protection against both sperm and sexually transmitted pathogens.
choice_rationales: ✘ Oral contraceptives prevent pregnancy but provide no protection against STIs.
✘ IUD effectively prevents pregnancy but offers no STI protection.
✔ Male and female condoms are the only contraceptive methods that prevent both pregnancy and STI transmission.
✘ Natural family planning has no contraceptive barrier and provides no STI protection.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: APGAR score assessed at?
questions.rationale: APGAR is assessed at 1 minute (immediate adaptation) and 5 minutes (response to resuscitation if needed).
choice_rationales: ✔ The 1-minute APGAR assesses immediate transition; 5-minute APGAR monitors response to any resuscitation.
✘ Waiting until 5 and 10 minutes misses the critical 1-minute assessment needed for immediate resuscitation decisions.
✘ 2 and 7 minutes are not the standard APGAR assessment times.
✘ 3 and 6 minutes are not the established APGAR assessment intervals.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Vitamin A deficiency primarily causes?
questions.rationale: Vitamin A is essential for rhodopsin synthesis in rod cells; deficiency causes night blindness and xerophthalmia.
choice_rationales: ✘ Rickets is caused by vitamin D deficiency, not vitamin A.
✔ Night blindness (nyctalopia) is the earliest sign of vitamin A deficiency; severe deficiency causes complete blindness.
✘ Scurvy is caused by vitamin C deficiency.
✘ Pellagra is caused by niacin (B3) deficiency.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Iodine deficiency causes?
questions.rationale: Without adequate iodine, the thyroid gland enlarges to capture more circulating iodine.
choice_rationales: ✔ Iodine is required for thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency causes compensatory thyroid gland enlargement (goiter).
✘ Anemia results from iron, folate, or B12 deficiency, not iodine.
✘ Night blindness results from vitamin A deficiency.
✘ Rickets results from vitamin D deficiency.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Rehabilitation is classified under which prevention level?
questions.rationale: Tertiary prevention minimizes disability and restores function after disease is established.
choice_rationales: ✘ Primary prevention prevents disease occurrence; rehabilitation occurs after disease.
✘ Secondary prevention detects and treats early disease; rehabilitation is for established disease.
✔ Tertiary prevention includes rehabilitation, limiting disability, and restoring maximum function.
✘ Primordial prevention addresses social and environmental conditions before risk factors develop — a newer concept not covering rehabilitation.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Immunity conferred by vaccines is called?
questions.rationale: Vaccines artificially stimulate the immune system to actively produce antibodies.
choice_rationales: ✘ Natural passive immunity is transferred via placenta or breast milk without deliberate administration.
✘ Natural active immunity results from recovering from actual infection, not vaccination.
✔ Artificial active immunity results from administration of an antigen (vaccine), stimulating the body to produce its own antibodies.
✘ Artificial passive immunity involves administering pre-formed antibodies (immunoglobulins), not stimulating antibody production.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Smallest political unit in the Philippines where health services are delivered?
questions.rationale: The barangay is the smallest administrative and political subdivision in the Philippines.
choice_rationales: ✘ The municipality encompasses multiple barangays; health services are delivered at the barangay level.
✘ The province encompasses multiple municipalities; it is not the smallest unit.
✔ The barangay is the basic political unit where BHWs, BHCs, and midwives deliver frontline health services.
✘ The region is the largest administrative division, encompassing multiple provinces.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Chairman of the Municipal Health Board?
questions.rationale: The Local Government Code designates the Municipal Mayor as the Chairman of the Municipal Health Board.
choice_rationales: ✔ RA 7160 (Local Government Code) establishes the Mayor as chairman of the Municipal Health Board.
✘ The Municipal Health Officer is a member of the board but not its chairman.
✘ The Public Health Nurse is a technical member; the mayor chairs the board.
✘ Any qualified physician is not the automatic chair; the mayor holds this position by law.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Public health nursing's most prominent feature?
choices: A. Provides home care to hospitalized patients
B. Services are free to all within catchment area
C. Nurse functions as part of a team
D. Focuses on preventive, not curative, services
questions.rationale: The defining characteristic of PHN is its primary focus on prevention and health promotion.
choice_rationales: ✘ Home care for hospitalized patients describes home health nursing, not the defining feature of PHN.
✘ Free services are a feature of government PHN but not its most prominent defining characteristic.
✘ Teamwork is important in all nursing; it is not the most prominent PHN characteristic.
✔ Preventive orientation — primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention — is the hallmark of public health nursing.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: When evaluating Ligtas Tigdas and resources were maximized — nurse is evaluating?
questions.rationale: Efficiency measures whether resources were used optimally to achieve program outcomes.
choice_rationales: ✘ Effectiveness measures whether the program achieved its intended outcomes, not resource utilization.
✔ Efficiency is the ratio of outputs to inputs — whether maximum results were obtained with minimum resources.
✘ Adequacy measures whether the program addressed the extent of the problem.
✘ Appropriateness measures whether the program fit the community's needs and values.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: New BSN graduate wanting to be a Public Health Nurse applies to?
questions.rationale: Public Health Nurses work at the RHU, the primary municipal-level community health facility.
choice_rationales: ✘ The DOH employs nurses at the national and regional levels, not frontline community positions.
✘ The Provincial Health Office supervises RHUs but does not directly employ PHNs for community work.
✘ Regional Health Offices manage regional programs; PHN positions are at the RHU.
✔ RHUs are the primary work setting for Public Health Nurses delivering community-based services.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Act mandating reporting of notifiable diseases?
questions.rationale: Act 3573 (Law on Reporting of Communicable Diseases) requires health workers to report notifiable diseases.
choice_rationales: ✔ Act 3573 mandates the reporting of communicable diseases to health authorities for surveillance and control.
✘ RA 3753 is the Civil Registry Law, not a communicable disease reporting law.
✘ RA 9173 is the Philippine Nursing Act of 2002.
✘ PD 996 provides for compulsory immunization against childhood diseases.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: In IMCI, which child has a DANGER SIGN requiring urgent referral?
choices: A. Child with mild diarrhea and adequate hydration
B. Child unable to drink or breastfeed
C. Child with fever for 2 days
questions.rationale: Inability to drink is an IMCI general danger sign indicating severe illness requiring immediate referral.
choice_rationales: ✘ Mild diarrhea with adequate hydration is managed at home with ORS; it is not a danger sign.
✔ IMCI general danger signs include inability to drink/breastfeed, convulsions, lethargy, and vomiting everything.
✘ Fever for 2 days requires further assessment but alone is not automatically a general danger sign.
✘ Mild cough without breathing difficulty is a non-urgent condition in IMCI classification.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Primary Health Care established by which declaration?
questions.rationale: The Alma Ata Declaration (1978) established PHC as the key strategy for Health for All.
choice_rationales: ✘ The Ottawa Charter (1986) defined health promotion; PHC was established earlier at Alma Ata.
✔ The Declaration of Alma Ata at the International Conference on PHC established the PHC approach globally.
✘ The Jakarta Declaration updated health promotion principles; it did not establish PHC.
✘ The Bangkok Charter addressed health promotion in a globalized world; PHC was established in 1978.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Dengue has 50 new cases from 10,000 population this month — incidence rate per 1,000?
questions.rationale: Incidence = (50/10,000) × 1,000 = 5 per 1,000 population.
choice_rationales: ✘ 0.5 would result if the denominator were 100,000 without adjustment.
✔ (50 ÷ 10,000) × 1,000 = 5 per 1,000 population per month.
✘ 50 would be the rate per 100,000 without the multiplication factor.
✘ 500 is a computational error using the wrong multiplier.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Infant mortality rate formula?
questions.rationale: IMR = (infant deaths under 1 year / live births) × 1,000 — measures risk of dying in first year.
choice_rationales: ✘ Using total population as the denominator gives a general mortality rate, not the infant-specific rate.
✔ Live births (not total population) is the correct denominator because it represents those at risk of dying in the first year.
✘ Using total deaths as denominator gives a proportionate mortality rate, not IMR.
✘ Neonatal deaths (under 28 days) calculate the neonatal mortality rate, not the infant mortality rate.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Attack rate is used during which disease occurrence pattern?
questions.rationale: Attack rate measures the proportion of exposed persons who develop disease in a defined epidemic event.
choice_rationales: ✘ Endemic describes constant background disease rates; attack rate is used for discrete outbreaks.
✘ Pandemic describes global spread; attack rate is used at the local outbreak level.
✔ Attack rate = (cases / exposed population) × 100; it quantifies the magnitude of a specific epidemic.
✘ Sporadic cases are isolated and unrelated; attack rate requires a defined exposure event.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Cholera cases cluster near a river — this represents which chain of infection component?
questions.rationale: The contaminated river serves as the reservoir (source) of Vibrio cholerae.
choice_rationales: ✘ The agent is Vibrio cholerae itself, not the water body.
✔ The river is the environmental reservoir harboring the pathogen; cases cluster because people use the contaminated water.
