✘ Mode of transmission is the fecal-oral route (drinking/touching contaminated water), not the river itself.
✘ Susceptible hosts are the community members who may become infected.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Windshield survey is a method of?
choices: B. Observational community assessment from a vehicle
questions.rationale: A windshield survey involves observing the community while driving through it to gather assessment data.
choice_rationales: ✘ Measuring blood pressure is a clinical activity, not a windshield survey.
✔ The nurse observes the community's physical, social, and environmental characteristics from a vehicle.
✘ Key informant interviews are a separate qualitative data collection method.
✘ Record review is a secondary data collection method, not a windshield survey.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Family with TB member undetected for months — which health task is NOT accomplished?
choices: A. Provide nursing care to sick member
C. Make decisions about health action
questions.rationale: Failure to detect TB signs and symptoms indicates the family has not accomplished health problem recognition.
choice_rationales: ✘ The family may be providing general care but this is not the failed task.
✔ Recognizing the signs of TB (cough >2 weeks, fever, night sweats) is the task the family failed to accomplish.
✘ Decision-making is a subsequent task; recognition must come first.
✘ Environmental modification comes after recognition and decision-making.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Grandmother giving rice water instead of ORS for diarrhea — most appropriate action?
choices: B. Educate on proper ORS preparation
questions.rationale: Health education empowers the caregiver with correct information while respecting the family's efforts.
choice_rationales: ✘ Reporting to the health center is premature; education is the first-line intervention.
✔ Health education about ORS preparation corrects the practice respectfully and builds on the grandmother's caregiving intention.
✘ IV fluids are for severe dehydration; the child is receiving some fluid, making education the priority.
✘ Criticizing the caregiver damages the therapeutic relationship and reduces her willingness to change.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Mother asks why child needs multiple OPV doses — correct explanation?
choices: A. Each dose targets a different disease
B. Multiple doses protect against all 3 poliovirus serotypes and overcome maternal antibody interference
C. The vaccine is weak and needs repetition
D. Multiple doses required only for malnourished children
questions.rationale: OPV covers 3 serotypes; multiple doses overcome maternal antibodies and ensure complete protection.
choice_rationales: ✘ OPV targets all 3 poliovirus serotypes in each dose, not different diseases.
✔ Multiple doses are needed because maternal antibodies can neutralize early doses and because each dose may not immunize against all 3 serotypes simultaneously.
✘ OPV is an effective vaccine; additional doses are given to ensure complete seroconversion, not because the vaccine is weak.
✘ Multiple doses are standard for all children regardless of nutritional status.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Minimum residual chlorine level for safe drinking water in the Philippines?
questions.rationale: PNSDW requires a minimum 0.5 mg/L residual chlorine for microbiological safety.
choice_rationales: ✘ 0.1 mg/L is below the Philippine standard and may not adequately disinfect the water.
✘ 0.3 mg/L is below the Philippine National Standards for Drinking Water requirement.
✔ 0.5 mg/L residual chlorine is the Philippine minimum standard ensuring pathogen destruction throughout the distribution system.
✘ 1.0 mg/L exceeds the minimum; while safe, it is not the stated minimum standard.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: LAM requires which three conditions?
choices: A. Exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhea, infant under 6 months
B. Partial breastfeeding, regular menstruation, infant under 1 year
C. Exclusive breastfeeding, regular cycles, infant under 12 months
D. Mixed feeding, amenorrhea, infant under 6 months
questions.rationale: All three conditions must be met simultaneously for LAM to be effective.
choice_rationales: ✔ LAM is 98% effective only when all three apply: exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhea, AND infant under 6 months.
✘ Partial breastfeeding does not provide adequate hormonal suppression; all three conditions including exclusive breastfeeding are needed.
✘ Regular menstrual cycles indicate the return of fertility; LAM is ineffective once menstruation resumes.
✘ Mixed feeding reduces suckling frequency, which is insufficient to maintain the hormonal suppression needed for LAM.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: 2-year-old child with weight-for-age Z-score of -3 SD indicates?
questions.rationale: WHO defines severe malnutrition as weight-for-age below -3 SD.
choice_rationales: ✘ Normal nutrition is between -2 and +2 SD; -3 SD is well below normal.
✘ Overweight is above +2 SD; -3 SD indicates severe undernutrition.
✔ A Z-score of -3 SD or below for weight-for-age indicates severe malnutrition requiring urgent therapeutic feeding.
✘ Mild malnutrition is between -1 and -2 SD; -3 SD indicates severe malnutrition.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Contact tracing best described as?
choices: A. Identifying persons who may have been exposed to an infectious case
B. Treating all community members with antibiotics
C. Closing schools during an epidemic
D. Reporting disease to the DOH
questions.rationale: Contact tracing identifies exposed persons to isolate, test, and break the chain of transmission.
choice_rationales: ✔ Contact tracing systematically identifies and monitors all persons who may have been exposed to an infectious case.
✘ Mass antibiotic treatment is mass chemoprophylaxis, not contact tracing.
✘ School closure is a social distancing measure, not contact tracing.
✘ Disease reporting is disease surveillance, separate from contact tracing.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: RA 7160 governs which aspect of health care delivery?
choices: B. Devolution of health services to LGUs
questions.rationale: RA 7160 (Local Government Code) devolved health service delivery authority to local government units.
choice_rationales: ✘ Nursing practice is governed by RA 9173 (Philippine Nursing Act of 2002).
✔ RA 7160 transferred responsibility for primary and secondary health services from national government to LGUs.
✘ Compulsory immunization is covered by PD 996.
✘ National health insurance is covered by RA 10606 (PhilHealth Act) and Universal Health Care Act.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Best study design for determining cause-and-effect between risk factor and disease?
questions.rationale: RCTs use randomization to eliminate confounding, providing the strongest evidence for causality.
choice_rationales: ✘ Cross-sectional studies measure prevalence at one point in time; they cannot establish temporal causality.
✘ Case-control studies identify risk factors retrospectively but are susceptible to recall and selection bias.
✔ The RCT's randomization creates comparable groups, making it the gold standard for establishing cause-and-effect.
✘ Ecological studies use population-level data and cannot infer individual-level causality (ecological fallacy).

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Ottawa Charter action area for health promotion?
questions.rationale: Building healthy public policy is one of the five Ottawa Charter action areas.
choice_rationales: ✘ Treating disease is curative care; the Ottawa Charter focuses on health promotion, not treatment.
✔ The five Ottawa Charter action areas: build healthy public policy, create supportive environments, strengthen community action, develop personal skills, reorient health services.
✘ Universal hospital care is a health system goal; the Ottawa Charter addresses upstream determinants.
✘ Distributing medications is a health service activity; the Charter addresses broader determinants.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Most environmentally acceptable solid waste disposal method?
questions.rationale: Sanitary landfills prevent groundwater contamination and disease vector proliferation.
choice_rationales: ✘ Open burning produces toxic air pollutants and greenhouse gases; it is environmentally harmful.
✔ Controlled sanitary landfills contain waste, manage leachate, and minimize environmental contamination.
✘ River dumping contaminates water supplies, destroys aquatic ecosystems, and violates environmental laws.
✘ Open dumping creates disease vectors (rats, flies), leachate contamination, and fire hazards.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: COPAR process begins with which phase?
questions.rationale: Integration is the first phase where health workers immerse in the community to build trust.
choice_rationales: ✘ Community organization follows after integration and community study.
✔ Integration (immersion) allows health workers to gain community trust before beginning formal organizing activities.
✘ The sustaining phase is the final phase of COPAR.
✘ Community study occurs after integration; initial immersion must establish rapport first.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Factory without occupational nurse — workers' occupational health needs served by?
choices: A. PHN at Provincial Health Office
C. PHN of the RHU of the municipality
D. Rural sanitary inspector of the RHU
questions.rationale: The PHN of the local RHU provides occupational health services to workers without their own nurse.
choice_rationales: ✘ The Provincial Health Office PHN works at the provincial level, not the local factory level.
✘ The factory physician provides clinical care but occupational health nursing is the PHN's scope.
✔ The municipal RHU PHN serves as the occupational health resource for workers in establishments without their own occupational health nurse.
✘ The rural sanitary inspector handles environmental sanitation, not occupational health nursing.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: TT immunization in pregnant women primarily protects against?
choices: D. Both neonatal and maternal tetanus
questions.rationale: TT protects the mother and transfers antibodies through the placenta to protect the newborn.
choice_rationales: ✘ TT protects the mother but also provides transplacental passive immunity to the newborn.
✘ Neonatal protection is critical but maternal protection is equally important.
✘ Older children are vaccinated directly; TT in pregnancy targets mother and newborn.
✔ TT during pregnancy creates immunity in the mother AND transfers protective antibodies across the placenta to prevent neonatal tetanus.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Teaching diabetic client insulin self-injection addresses which learning domain?
questions.rationale: Psychomotor learning involves physical skill acquisition through hands-on practice.
choice_rationales: ✘ Cognitive learning involves knowledge and understanding (e.g., explaining insulin mechanisms).
✘ Affective learning involves attitudes and values (e.g., accepting the need for insulin).
✔ Insulin self-injection is a physical skill requiring psychomotor learning through guided practice and return demonstration.
✘ Evaluative is not a standard learning domain; Bloom's taxonomy includes cognitive, affective, and psychomotor.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Safe postpartum family planning method to start immediately after delivery?
questions.rationale: Progestin-only pills are safe immediately postpartum and do not suppress lactation.
choice_rationales: ✘ Combined oral contraceptives containing estrogen inhibit milk production and are not recommended before 6 weeks postpartum.
✘ IUD insertion is safe immediately postpartum (within 48 hours or after 4 weeks) but not throughout the postpartum period.
✔ Progestin-only pills can be started immediately after delivery; they do not contain estrogen that suppresses lactation.
✘ BBT method cannot be used during lactational amenorrhea because the temperature pattern is unreliable.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: TB transmitted via?
choices: B. Inhalation of airborne droplet nuclei
questions.rationale: Mycobacterium tuberculosis spreads through airborne droplet nuclei released by infectious persons.
choice_rationales: ✘ Skin contact does not transmit tuberculosis; it is an airborne disease.
✔ Droplet nuclei containing TB bacilli remain suspended in the air and are inhaled by susceptible persons.
✘ TB is not foodborne or waterborne; it requires inhalation of infected particles.
✘ Insects do not transmit tuberculosis; no vector is involved.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Municipality of 20,000 with 3 midwives — how many more midwife items needed?
questions.rationale: DOH standard: 1 midwife per 5,000 population. 20,000 ÷ 5,000 = 4 needed; 4 − 3 = 1 more.
choice_rationales: ✔ The DOH staffing norm requires 4 midwives for 20,000 population; with 3 on staff, 1 additional midwife item is needed.
✘ 2 more would imply only 2 midwives are currently on staff.
✘ 3 more would imply no midwives are currently on staff.
✘ The RHU needs 1 more midwife to meet the DOH staffing standard.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Core group formation in COPAR involves?
questions.rationale: Core group formation identifies and trains community members who will lead health programs.
choice_rationales: ✘ Integration is the first phase of COPAR involving immersion, not core group formation.
✘ Community organization is a broader phase; core group formation is a specific step within it.
✘ Community study is the assessment phase; core group formation is an organizing activity.
✔ Core group formation involves identifying, recruiting, and training community members who become the foundation for sustained health organizing.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse uses key informant interview — this is?
questions.rationale: Key informant interviews yield narrative, in-depth qualitative information about community issues.
choice_rationales: ✘ Quantitative methods use numbers and statistical analysis; interviews yield qualitative data.
✔ Key informant interviews provide rich, contextual qualitative data about community perceptions, priorities, and experiences.
✘ Secondary data review involves analyzing existing records, not conducting interviews.
✘ Laboratory data is biological specimen analysis, not community assessment.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: First-line control for excessive workplace noise?
choices: C. Engineering controls to eliminate or reduce noise
D. Transfer workers to quiet areas
questions.rationale: Engineering controls at the source are the highest priority in the hierarchy of hazard controls.
choice_rationales: ✘ Earplugs are personal protective equipment (PPE) — the last resort in hazard control hierarchy.
✘ Hearing tests monitor for hearing loss but do not control the noise hazard.
✔ The hierarchy of controls prioritizes elimination/substitution and engineering controls before administrative controls or PPE.
✘ Job transfer is an administrative control — the third level in the hierarchy, after engineering controls.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: D in DOTS stands for?
questions.rationale: DOTS = Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course — observation is the key strategy.
choice_rationales: ✘ Diagnosis begins the TB care pathway but is not what D stands for in DOTS.
✔ Direct observation means a health worker watches the patient swallow every dose, ensuring adherence.
✘ Discharge is not part of the DOTS acronym.
✘ Disease is not part of the DOTS acronym.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: PHN computes maternal mortality ratio with 5 deaths and 2,000 live births — MMR per 100,000?
choice_rationales: ✘ 25 would result from (5/2,000) × 10,000; the standard denominator is 100,000.
✔ (5 ÷ 2,000) × 100,000 = 250 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births.
✘ 2,500 would result from (5/2,000) × 1,000,000 — using the wrong multiplier.
✘ 0.25 is the simple proportion without multiplying by 100,000.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: RA 10028 mandates promotion of which infant feeding practice?
choices: B. Formula feeding for working mothers
C. Exclusive breastfeeding for first 6 months
questions.rationale: RA 10028 (Expanded Breastfeeding Promotion Act) mandates promotion of exclusive breastfeeding.
choice_rationales: ✘ Mixed feeding is discouraged by RA 10028; exclusive breastfeeding is mandated.
✘ Formula feeding promotion in health facilities is prohibited by the Milk Code and RA 10028.
✔ RA 10028 requires all health facilities to promote and support exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months.
✘ Supplemental feeding before 6 months violates the exclusive breastfeeding mandate.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Pre-placement examination PRIMARY purpose?
choices: A. Screen for pre-existing conditions that may affect job performance or be aggravated by work
B. Diagnose and treat acute illness
questions.rationale: Pre-placement exams match workers to appropriate jobs based on their health status.
choice_rationales: ✔ Pre-placement exams identify conditions that could be worsened by specific work exposures, protecting both worker and employer.
✘ Treating illness is a clinical function; pre-placement exams are occupational fitness assessments.
✘ Legal documentation is a secondary benefit; the primary purpose is worker-job matching.
✘ Productivity measurement is an HR function, not the purpose of a health examination.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Point-source epidemic with cases peaking within one incubation period indicates?
questions.rationale: A sharp peak within one incubation period means all cases were exposed simultaneously at one source.
choice_rationales: ✘ Continuous exposure causes cases to spread over multiple incubation periods, not a single peak.
✘ Person-to-person transmission creates a propagated epidemic with multiple waves, not a single peak.
✔ All cases being exposed at the same time and place (e.g., contaminated food at a banquet) creates the characteristic single-peak epidemic curve.
✘ A propagated epidemic shows multiple peaks as each wave of cases transmits to new hosts.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: PHC principle addressing health disparities from socioeconomic factors?
questions.rationale: Equitable distribution ensures health resources reach underserved communities to reduce disparities.
choice_rationales: ✘ Appropriate technology ensures methods fit the community context but doesn't directly address disparities.
✘ Multi-sectoral approach involves multiple sectors collaborating but doesn't specifically address inequitable distribution.
✘ Community participation involves people in health decisions but doesn't directly address resource equity.
✔ Equitable distribution prioritizes resources toward those with greatest need to reduce health disparities.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Ring vaccination during measles outbreak targets?
choices: A. All susceptible individuals in the municipality
C. Contacts and close contacts of confirmed cases
questions.rationale: Ring vaccination creates a protective immune buffer around confirmed cases.
choice_rationales: ✘ Mass vaccination covers all susceptibles; ring vaccination is more targeted and resource-efficient.
✘ Cases are already infected; vaccination of cases does not prevent further spread.
✔ Ring vaccination immunizes the social network around each case, creating an immune barrier that interrupts onward transmission.
✘ Immunocompromised individuals may receive special vaccines but are not the exclusive target of ring vaccination.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Highest PATCH priority score?
choices: A. High prevalence, low changeability, moderate concern
B. Low prevalence, high changeability, low concern
C. High prevalence, high changeability, high community concern
questions.rationale: All three PATCH criteria high gives the maximum priority score.
choice_rationales: ✘ Low changeability means the problem is difficult to address; it should not receive the highest priority.
✘ Low prevalence and low community concern reduce the priority score significantly.
✔ PATCH prioritizes problems that are common (high prevalence), amenable to intervention (high changeability), and community-motivated (high concern).
✘ Moderate scores in all factors give a medium priority, not the highest.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: COC user presents with sudden headache, visual disturbance, and one-sided weakness — nurse suspects?
choices: B. Thromboembolic stroke from estrogen effect
questions.rationale: Estrogen in COCs increases clotting risk; sudden neurological symptoms indicate thromboembolic event.
choice_rationales: ✘ Pill withdrawal headaches are mild and cyclical, not accompanied by focal neurological deficits.
✔ Estrogen increases coagulation factors; arterial thromboembolism causing stroke presents with sudden neurological deficits.
✘ Progesterone-related headaches are not associated with focal neurological signs.
✘ COC-related hypertension causes gradual pressure changes, not sudden focal neurological symptoms.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Prevalence 200/1,000 and incidence 40/1,000 per year — ratio suggests?
questions.rationale: Duration = Prevalence/Incidence = 200/40 = 5 years; cases persist, accumulating in the population.
choice_rationales: ✘ Short duration would show a low prevalence relative to incidence.
✔ The relationship P = I × D allows calculation: 200/40 = 5 years average disease duration.
✘ Rapidly fatal diseases show low prevalence despite high incidence because cases die quickly.
✘ The calculation gives duration, not a trend in incidence.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Measure of association in a case-control study?
questions.rationale: Odds ratio is the appropriate measure in case-control studies because incidence cannot be calculated.
choice_rationales: ✘ Relative risk requires incidence data from cohort or experimental studies; it cannot be directly calculated in case-control designs.
✔ Odds ratio compares the odds of exposure between cases and controls, estimating relative risk when disease is rare.
✘ Attributable risk requires incidence data available from cohort studies.
✘ Attack rate is used during epidemics with defined exposure events, not in case-control designs.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Screening test correctly identifies 90% of true TB cases — this value is?
questions.rationale: Sensitivity is the proportion of true positives correctly identified by the test.
choice_rationales: ✘ Specificity is the proportion of truly disease-free people correctly identified as negative.
✘ Positive predictive value is the probability that a positive test result is truly positive; it depends on prevalence.
✔ Sensitivity = true positives / (true positives + false negatives) × 100; 90% sensitivity means 10% of true cases are missed.
✘ Negative predictive value is the probability that a negative result is truly negative.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: High specificity but low sensitivity test — means?
choices: A. Many true cases missed but false positives are rare
B. All true cases identified but many false positives
C. Equal ability to detect and rule out disease
D. High PPV in all populations
questions.rationale: High specificity = few false positives; low sensitivity = many false negatives (missed cases).
choice_rationales: ✔ A highly specific test rarely misclassifies healthy people as sick (few false positives) but misses many true cases (low sensitivity).
✘ Identifying all true cases describes high sensitivity, not high specificity.
✘ Equal ability to detect and rule out would require both high sensitivity AND high specificity.
✘ PPV depends on both specificity AND disease prevalence; high specificity alone doesn't guarantee high PPV.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Swaroop's index measures the proportion of deaths occurring in persons aged?
questions.rationale: Swaroop's index = (deaths aged ≥50 / total deaths) × 100; high index indicates good health development.
choice_rationales: ✘ Deaths under 5 are captured by the under-5 mortality rate; Swaroop uses age 50+.
✔ A high Swaroop's index means most deaths occur after age 50, indicating people live to old age — a sign of good population health.
✘ Deaths in the 15–49 age group represent working-age mortality, not Swaroop's index.
✘ Using age 65+ is not the definition of Swaroop's index, which uses age 50+.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: TLV-TWA represents?
choices: A. Maximum safe concentration for 15-minute exposure
B. Average airborne concentration for 8-hour day/40-hour week considered safe for lifetime occupational exposure
C. Concentration immediately dangerous to life and health
D. Concentration causing skin irritation in 50% of workers
questions.rationale: TLV-TWA is the 8-hour average airborne concentration considered safe for daily repeated exposure.
choice_rationales: ✘ A 15-minute maximum describes TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit), not TLV-TWA.
✔ TLV-TWA is the time-weighted average over an 8-hour day and 40-hour week that nearly all workers can tolerate without adverse effects throughout their working lifetime.
✘ IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) is a separate, much higher concentration value.
✘ Concentration causing skin irritation in 50% is not a standard TLV definition.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Outbreak: 15/80 ate egg salad got sick, 2/60 who didn't eat got sick — relative risk?
choice_rationales: ✘ 3.6 results from incorrect calculation of the attack rates.
✔ RR = (15/80) ÷ (2/60) = 0.1875 ÷ 0.0333 = 5.6; egg salad eaters were 5.6 times more likely to develop illness.
✘ 7.5 overestimates the risk; recalculation is needed.
✘ 2.8 underestimates the risk; the correct calculation gives 5.6.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Family fails to seek care despite obvious illness due to distrust — which health task is most impaired?
choices: B. Making decisions about appropriate health action
C. Providing nursing care to sick member
questions.rationale: The family recognizes illness but chooses not to act — decision-making is the impaired task.
choice_rationales: ✘ Recognition has occurred (they know someone is ill); the failure is in acting on that recognition.
✔ Decision-making about appropriate health action is impaired when distrust, cultural beliefs, or cost prevent the family from seeking care despite recognizing illness.
✘ Providing home care may be ongoing; the specific failure is not seeking professional care.
✘ Environmental maintenance is a separate health task not directly related to care-seeking.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: After confirming diagnosis and establishing case definitions in outbreak investigation, next step?
choices: C. Describe cases by person, place, and time
D. Identify causative agent in laboratory
questions.rationale: Systematic case description generates hypotheses about the source and transmission of the outbreak.
choice_rationales: ✘ Control measures are implemented after generating hypotheses from case description.
✘ The report is written after completing the investigation.
✔ Descriptive epidemiology (person, place, time) identifies patterns that generate hypotheses about the outbreak source.
✘ Laboratory identification confirms the agent but descriptive epidemiology provides the context needed to guide control.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Draining stagnant water for malaria prevention targets which Leavell-Clark model component?
questions.rationale: Stagnant water is an environmental factor supporting mosquito breeding; modifying it breaks transmission.
choice_rationales: ✘ The agent (Plasmodium) would be targeted by antimalarial drugs, not drainage.
✘ The vector would be targeted by insecticides or biological control; drainage targets the breeding environment.
✘ The host would be targeted by prophylaxis or bed nets; drainage targets the environment.
✔ Draining stagnant water modifies the environment, eliminating mosquito breeding sites and breaking the transmission cycle.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Delphi technique involves?
choices: A. Random sampling of community members for surveys
B. Multiple rounds of questionnaires for anonymous expert consensus
D. Focus group discussion with leaders
questions.rationale: Delphi uses iterative questionnaire rounds with anonymized feedback to reach consensus.
choice_rationales: ✘ Random sampling is a quantitative survey method, not the Delphi technique.
✔ The Delphi method iteratively collects and summarizes expert opinions anonymously until consensus is reached.
✘ Direct observation is a community assessment method, not the Delphi technique.
✘ Focus groups gather diverse opinions simultaneously; Delphi uses anonymous sequential rounds.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Proportionate mortality rate (PMR) measures?
choices: A. Death rate from a disease per 100,000 population
B. Proportion of all deaths attributed to a specific cause
C. Incidence of a disease in the population
D. Case fatality rate from a disease
questions.rationale: PMR = (deaths from specific cause / total deaths) × 100 — proportion, not rate.
choice_rationales: ✘ Death rate per 100,000 is a cause-specific mortality rate, not PMR.
✔ PMR shows what fraction of all deaths are due to a specific condition, useful for comparing disease burden.
✘ Incidence measures new cases per population, not the proportion of deaths.
✘ Case fatality rate measures deaths among diagnosed cases of a disease, not proportion of all deaths.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Water source MPN coliform = 10 per 100 mL indicates?
choices: B. Unsafe — acceptable MPN is less than 1.1 per 100 mL
questions.rationale: PNSDW requires MPN <1.1/100 mL; 10/100 mL indicates significant fecal contamination.
choice_rationales: ✘ The water is NOT safe; 10/100 mL greatly exceeds the maximum allowable coliform count.
✔ Philippine standards require MPN of coliforms below 1.1 per 100 mL; 10/100 mL indicates substantial fecal contamination.
✘ The elevated count is dangerous for all users, not just immunocompromised individuals.
✘ The result is clearly abnormal; repeat testing is not needed before advising against use.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Family coping index of 3 on first contact indicates?
choices: A. Excellent coping needing minimal intervention
B. Moderate problems requiring some guidance
C. Severe dysfunction requiring intensive nursing support
questions.rationale: In the Family Coping Index (1–5 scale), a score of 3 indicates moderate problems.
choice_rationales: ✘ Excellent coping (score 4–5) requires minimal intervention; score 3 indicates moderate need.
✔ A coping index score of 3 on the 1–5 scale reflects moderate problems and guides the nurse to provide structured guidance and support.
✘ Severe dysfunction is reflected by scores of 1–2 requiring intensive support; score 3 is moderate.
✘ A score of 3 indicates identified problems; no problems would score 4–5.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Hepatitis A outbreak traced to infected food handler — food handler represents?
questions.rationale: The infected food handler is the source/reservoir of hepatitis A virus.
choice_rationales: ✔ The food handler harboring hepatitis A virus is the reservoir; contaminated food is the vehicle of transmission.
✘ Portal of exit is the route through which the agent leaves the reservoir (fecal-oral in this case).
✘ The susceptible host is the unvaccinated diner who consumes contaminated food.
✘ Mode of transmission is the fecal-oral route via contaminated food, not the food handler themselves.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: HIGHEST musculoskeletal injury risk ergonomic hazard?
choices: B. Workers repeatedly lifting 30 kg from floor level without mechanical aids
C. Workers standing on anti-fatigue mats for 4 hours
D. Workers rotating tasks every 2 hours
questions.rationale: Heavy loads + awkward posture + high frequency = maximum musculoskeletal injury risk.
choice_rationales: ✘ Adjustable desks are an ergonomic control that reduces injury risk.
✔ Repetitive heavy lifting from floor level combines three major injury risk factors: heavy load, extreme awkward posture (stooping), and high frequency.
✘ Anti-fatigue mats reduce lower extremity fatigue; standing for 4 hours with mats is manageable.
✘ Task rotation is an administrative control that reduces repetitive strain — it decreases injury risk.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: 'Iceberg phenomenon' in disease surveillance explains that?
choices: A. Most diseases are visible and easily diagnosed
B. Official case counts represent only a fraction of total disease burden
C. Prevention programs eliminate all undetected cases
D. Surveillance systems capture all community cases
questions.rationale: The visible tip of the iceberg represents reported cases; most disease is undetected below the surface.
choice_rationales: ✘ Most infectious disease cases are NOT visible to surveillance; subclinical and unreported cases predominate.
✔ The iceberg model illustrates that reported cases are a small fraction; mild, asymptomatic, and undiagnosed cases represent the vast hidden burden.
✘ Prevention programs reduce incidence; they cannot eliminate already undetected past cases.
✘ Surveillance systems capture only a fraction of true disease burden; the iceberg phenomenon describes this gap.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Attributable risk is calculated as?
choices: A. Incidence in exposed minus incidence in unexposed
B. Incidence in exposed divided by incidence in unexposed
C. Odds ratio adjusted for confounders
D. Prevalence in exposed minus prevalence in unexposed
questions.rationale: Attributable risk (risk difference) measures excess risk due to the exposure.
choice_rationales: ✔ Attributable risk = incidence exposed − incidence unexposed; it quantifies the absolute excess risk from the exposure.
✘ Dividing incidences gives relative risk (ratio), not attributable risk (difference).
✘ Odds ratio is a relative measure used in case-control studies, not absolute attributable risk.
✘ Prevalence difference gives the population impact but attributable risk specifically uses incidence data.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Researcher follows 1,000 smokers and 1,000 non-smokers for 10 years — study type?
questions.rationale: Following defined exposed and unexposed groups over time to compare disease incidence is a prospective cohort design.
choice_rationales: ✘ Case-control studies start with disease status and look backward at exposure; this study follows forward.
✘ Cross-sectional studies measure exposure and disease simultaneously at one point in time.
✔ Prospective cohort studies follow disease-free groups with different exposures over time, comparing incidence rates.
✘ RCTs involve random assignment of an intervention; smokers are not randomly assigned — it is an observational cohort study.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Purpose of Philippine Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) in health?
choices: A. Determine tax liability of industrial projects
B. Assess potential health and environmental effects before project implementation
C. Monitor post-operational pollution levels only
D. Issue permits for waste disposal
questions.rationale: EIA evaluates potential health and environmental impacts of proposed projects before approval.
choice_rationales: ✘ EIA is an environmental health tool, not a tax assessment mechanism.
✔ The Philippine EIA system requires developers to assess and mitigate potential environmental and health impacts before project implementation.
✘ Post-operational monitoring is part of ongoing compliance, but EIA's primary function is pre-implementation assessment.
✘ Waste disposal permits are issued by DENR; EIA is a broader environmental review process.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: CATCH method in community assessment stands for?
choices: A. Community Assessment Techniques for Community Health
B. Categories for Assessing Target Community Health
C. Compile, Analyze, Target, Change, Help
D. Community Assets, Territory, Concerns, History
questions.rationale: CATCH (Community Assets, Territory, Concerns, History) provides a framework for comprehensive community assessment.
choice_rationales: ✘ This is a plausible-sounding alternative but not the standard CATCH framework.
✘ This also sounds reasonable but does not reflect the standard framework.
✘ This action-oriented sequence is not the CATCH assessment framework.
✔ The CATCH framework systematically assesses community assets (strengths), territory (physical environment), concerns (health problems), and history (past experiences).

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: 3 Cs of dengue prevention and control?
questions.rationale: Check for breeding sites, Clear/empty containers, Cover water containers — the 3 C's of dengue prevention.
choice_rationales: ✘ Clean, Cover, Chemically treat is a water sanitation approach, not the dengue 3 Cs.
✘ Chlorinate, Control, Contain is not the standard dengue prevention framework.
✔ The 3 Cs: Check for stagnant water, Clear/empty containers, Cover water storage — the standard household dengue prevention strategy.
✘ Catch, Contain, Clean is not the standard dengue prevention framework.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Reducing over-medicalization is the goal of which prevention level?
questions.rationale: Quaternary prevention protects patients from iatrogenic harm and over-diagnosis.
choice_rationales: ✘ Primary prevention prevents disease onset; it does not address over-medicalization.
✘ Secondary prevention detects early disease; it does not prevent over-treatment.
✘ Tertiary prevention rehabilitates after disease; it does not address unnecessary medical interventions.
✔ Quaternary prevention — a relatively newer concept — protects people from interventions that are likely to cause more harm than good (over-diagnosis, over-treatment).

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Comprehensive community assessment framework — FIVE A's?
choices: A. Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability, Appropriateness of services
B. Assessment, Analysis, Action, Accountability, Advocacy
C. Age, Address, Attitude, Access, Adherence
D. Acute, Ambulatory, Administrative, Aggregate, Accessible
questions.rationale: The 5 A's assess whether health services are available, accessible, affordable, acceptable, and appropriate.
choice_rationales: ✔ The 5 A's framework evaluates whether health services meet community needs in terms of availability, physical access, cost, cultural acceptability, and appropriateness.
✘ This describes nursing process steps, not the 5 A's community assessment framework.
✘ These are demographic and behavioral factors, not the 5 A's service assessment framework.
✘ These describe types of care settings, not the 5 A's community health framework.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Primary prevention in occupational health is best exemplified by?
choices: B. Replacing toxic chemical with a safer substance
C. Providing rehabilitation after occupational injury
questions.rationale: Substituting a hazardous substance eliminates the risk before harm occurs — primary prevention.
choice_rationales: ✘ Pre-employment exams screen existing conditions; they do not prevent occupational hazard exposure.
✔ Substituting a toxic chemical with a safer alternative eliminates the hazard at the source — the highest level of primary prevention.
✘ Rehabilitation after injury is tertiary prevention.
✘ Periodic medical surveillance detects early effects — secondary prevention.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Epidemiological triad applied to pneumonia — treatment with antibiotics targets which component?
questions.rationale: Antibiotics target the bacterial agent (Streptococcus pneumoniae, etc.) causing the infection.
choice_rationales: ✘ Host interventions would include improving immunity (vaccination) or health status (nutrition).
✔ Antibiotics are antimicrobial agents that kill or inhibit bacterial pathogens — directly targeting the agent in the triad.
✘ Environmental interventions include reducing crowding and improving ventilation.
✘ Pneumonia does not involve a vector; it is spread by respiratory droplets.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse uses PATCH model to prioritize — which criterion measures ease of change?
questions.rationale: Changeability (modifiability) assesses how amenable the health problem is to intervention.
choice_rationales: ✘ Prevalence measures how widespread the problem is in the population.
✔ Changeability assesses whether effective interventions exist and whether implementation is feasible — a key PATCH priority criterion.
✘ Community concern measures how important the problem is to community members.
✘ Severity is not one of the three standard PATCH criteria (prevalence, changeability, community concern).

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Class A water supply in the Philippines means?
choices: A. Water from a deep well without treatment
B. Level 1 water system serving fewer than 15 households from a point source
C. Level 3 individual household water connection from a piped system
questions.rationale: Class A (Level 3) water supply is a fully waterworks system with individual household connections.
choice_rationales: ✘ Deep wells without treatment are typically Level 1 or 2 systems, not Class A.
✘ Level 1 (point source) serves a communal faucet; Class A/Level 3 provides individual connections.
✔ Class A/Level 3 water supply provides individual piped connections to each household — the highest service level in Philippine water supply classification.
✘ Rainwater collection (Level 1) is the lowest service level, not Class A.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Standard treatment duration for drug-susceptible TB under DOTS?
questions.rationale: Standard TB treatment is 2 months intensive phase + 4 months continuation phase = 6 months total.
choice_rationales: ✘ 2 months is only the intensive phase; treatment continues for 4 more months.
✘ 4 months represents only the continuation phase; total treatment is 6 months.
✔ The WHO-recommended standard short-course treatment for drug-susceptible TB is 6 months total (2HRZE/4HR).
✘ 12 months was the older treatment regimen; modern DOTS uses 6-month short-course treatment.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Nurse computes specific mortality rate for cardiovascular disease — formula?
questions.rationale: Specific mortality rate = (deaths from specific cause / population at risk) × 100,000.
choice_rationales: ✘ Using total deaths as denominator gives proportionate mortality rate (PMR), not specific mortality rate.
✔ Cause-specific mortality rate uses population at risk as denominator, giving the probability of dying from that cause.
✘ Using live births as denominator calculates infant/neonatal mortality rates, not cause-specific rates.
✘ Using cases in the numerator gives case fatality rate (CFR), not mortality rate.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Freeman and Heinrich state CHN is a developmental service — best illustration?
choices: A. CHN nurse continuously develops personally and professionally
B. Health education and community organizing are necessary in providing services
C. CHN is primarily for health promotion and prevention
D. CHN provides nursing to people in their residences
questions.rationale: Developmental means building community capacity through education and organizing for sustained change.
choice_rationales: ✘ Personal professional development of the nurse describes individual growth, not the developmental nature of the service.
✔ Health education and community organizing develop the community's capacity to sustain health improvements beyond the nurse's intervention.
✘ Health promotion focus describes PHN philosophy, not specifically what makes it developmental.
✘ Home-based service delivery is a characteristic of CHN but not the definition of developmental service.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: Philippines declared which disease target for eradication by Presidential Proclamation No. 4?
questions.rationale: Presidential Proclamation No. 4 declared measles as a target for eradication in the Philippines.
choice_rationales: ✘ Polio eradication was declared by a different proclamation; PP No. 4 is specifically for measles.
✔ Presidential Proclamation No. 4 specifically targets measles for eradication due to its preventability and high childhood burden.
✘ Rabies control is addressed through separate legislation and programs.
✘ Neonatal tetanus elimination is a goal under the maternal-neonatal tetanus elimination program, not PP No. 4.

questions.subject: Community Health Nursing
questions.stem: According to C.E. Winslow, goal of public health is?
choices: B. Promotion of health and prevention of disease
C. People to have access to basic health services
D. For people to attain their birthrights of health and longevity
questions.rationale: Winslow defined public health's ultimate goal as enabling people to live out their full health potential.
choice_rationales: ✘ 'Health for All Filipinos' is the DOH vision/slogan, not Winslow's definition.
✘ Health promotion and disease prevention are means to Winslow's goal, not the goal itself.
✘ Access to basic services is a strategy, not Winslow's definition of the goal of public health.
✔ Winslow (1920) defined public health as the art of preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health to enable people to attain their birthrights of health and longevity.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: An antidepressant primarily increases which neurotransmitter to improve mood?
choices: A. Serotonin in the synaptic cleft
B. Dopamine in the nigrostriatal pathway
C. Acetylcholine in the neuromuscular junction
D. GABA in the limbic system
questions.rationale: Low serotonin is strongly associated with depression; SSRIs block reuptake to increase synaptic serotonin.
choice_rationales: ✔ SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) prevent serotonin reabsorption, increasing its availability in the synapse and improving mood.
✘ Dopamine in the nigrostriatal pathway is associated with motor control (Parkinson's) and the reward system, not the primary mechanism of antidepressants.
✘ Acetylcholine in the neuromuscular junction controls muscle contraction; it is unrelated to mood regulation.
✘ GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter; benzodiazepines enhance GABA, reducing anxiety, but this is not the primary antidepressant mechanism.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Auditory hallucinations in schizophrenia are most associated with?
questions.rationale: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia (hallucinations, delusions) result from dopamine hyperactivity in mesolimbic pathways.
choice_rationales: ✘ Decreased dopamine activity causes negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia) and motor side effects of antipsychotics.
✔ Dopamine hypothesis: excess dopaminergic activity in mesolimbic pathways produces positive psychotic symptoms.
✘ Increased GABA activity is inhibitory and associated with reduced anxiety, not hallucinations.
✘ Decreased norepinephrine is associated with depression, not schizophrenic hallucinations.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: A client with persistent anxiety and restlessness — anticipated neurotransmitter imbalance?
questions.rationale: GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter; low GABA reduces inhibition and causes anxiety.
choice_rationales: ✘ Increased serotonin is associated with improved mood; decreased serotonin is linked to anxiety and depression.
✘ Acetylcholine imbalances are associated with Alzheimer's disease and myasthenia gravis, not primarily anxiety.
✔ GABA inhibits neuronal excitability; deficiency allows unchecked neural firing, producing anxiety and restlessness.
✘ Increased GABA would have an anxiolytic (calming) effect, the opposite of anxiety.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which statement indicates correct understanding of neurotransmitters?
choices: D. Serotonin regulates mood and sleep
questions.rationale: Serotonin modulates mood, sleep, appetite, memory, and social behavior.
choice_rationales: ✘ Dopamine increases pleasure and reward; its deficiency is associated with anhedonia and depression.
✘ GABA is the primary INHIBITORY neurotransmitter; it reduces neural excitability.
✘ Norepinephrine INCREASES alertness and arousal; reduced NE is associated with depression and fatigue.
✔ Serotonin regulates mood, sleep cycles, appetite, pain perception, and social functioning.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client newly prescribed Fluoxetine asks when it will work — best response?
choices: B. 'Effects are usually seen within 2–4 weeks.'
C. 'Take double the dose if no effect after 1 week.'
D. 'Stop if no improvement after 3 days.'
questions.rationale: SSRIs require 2–4 weeks for therapeutic effects to develop as receptor downregulation occurs.
choice_rationales: ✘ SSRIs do not work immediately; patients must be counseled about the delay to prevent premature discontinuation.
✔ The therapeutic lag of 2–4 weeks occurs because receptor changes require time to produce clinical antidepressant effects.
✘ Doubling the dose is dangerous and not evidence-based; dose adjustments require medical authorization.
✘ Stopping after 3 days is premature and can cause discontinuation syndrome; 2–4 weeks are needed.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client on Sertraline reports decreased libido — nurse's best action?
choices: C. Reassure that this is a common side effect
questions.rationale: Sexual dysfunction is a well-documented, common SSRI side effect that should be acknowledged.
choice_rationales: ✘ Discontinuing medication without medical consultation risks relapse of depression.
✘ There is no antidote for SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction; dose adjustment or medication switch may be discussed with the provider.
✔ Sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, delayed orgasm) affects up to 40–60% of patients on SSRIs; acknowledging this normalizes the experience and maintains therapeutic alliance.
✘ Ignoring a patient concern violates therapeutic communication principles and patient rights.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which antidepressant teaching statement requires correction?
choices: A. 'I should avoid alcohol while taking this medication.'
B. 'I may experience mild side effects initially.'
C. 'I need to take this medication regularly.'
D. 'I can stop the medication once I feel better.'
questions.rationale: Abrupt discontinuation of antidepressants causes relapse and discontinuation syndrome.
choice_rationales: ✘ Avoiding alcohol is correct — alcohol is a CNS depressant that worsens depression and interacts with antidepressants.
✘ Expecting mild initial side effects (nausea, headache) is correct; these usually resolve with continued use.
✘ Regular daily dosing maintains therapeutic blood levels; this is correct teaching.
✔ Stopping when feeling better is INCORRECT; antidepressants should be continued for 6–12 months minimum to prevent relapse.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client on SSRIs develops agitation, confusion, and sweating — nurse suspects?
questions.rationale: The classic triad of serotonin syndrome: altered mental status, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular abnormalities.
choice_rationales: ✔ Serotonin syndrome occurs from excess serotonergic activity; triad includes agitation/confusion, diaphoresis/tachycardia, and tremor/clonus.
✘ Neuroleptic malignant syndrome occurs with antipsychotics and presents with hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, and altered consciousness.
✘ Hypertensive crisis (MAOIs + tyramine) presents with severe headache and hypertension, not this constellation of symptoms.
✘ SSRI discontinuation syndrome causes flu-like symptoms and 'electric shock' sensations, not the acute triad described.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client states 'The TV is sending me messages' — nurse's best response?
choices: B. 'The TV cannot send messages, but I understand you feel that way.'
questions.rationale: Therapeutic communication acknowledges the client's feelings while gently presenting reality.
choice_rationales: ✘ Flatly contradicting delusions creates conflict and damages trust without therapeutic benefit.
✔ This response validates the emotional experience without reinforcing the delusion — the therapeutic approach to psychotic symptoms.
✘ Asking 'why' invites the client to elaborate on the delusion, potentially reinforcing delusional thinking.
✘ Agreeing with the delusion reinforces it and is therapeutically harmful.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client has command hallucinations instructing self-harm — priority action?
questions.rationale: Safety is always the highest priority when a client is at immediate risk of self-harm.
choice_rationales: ✔ Command hallucinations instructing self-harm create an immediate safety emergency; the nurse must intervene to protect the client.
✘ Documentation is important but safety must be secured first.
✘ PRN medication may be administered, but ensuring immediate safety takes precedence.
✘ Reorientation is appropriate for confusion; command hallucinations require safety intervention first.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which behavior is a NEGATIVE symptom of schizophrenia?
questions.rationale: Negative symptoms represent deficits in normal function: flat affect, alogia, avolition, and social withdrawal.
choice_rationales: ✘ Auditory hallucinations are positive symptoms — added experiences not normally present.
✘ Delusions are positive symptoms — fixed false beliefs added to normal cognition.
✔ Social withdrawal is a negative symptom reflecting reduced motivation and social engagement.
✘ Disorganized speech is a positive symptom — abnormal added behavior not normally present.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Schizophrenia client is withdrawn and refuses to talk — most appropriate approach?
questions.rationale: Simple, brief communication reduces cognitive overload and anxiety in withdrawn schizophrenic clients.
choice_rationales: ✘ Ignoring the client abandons therapeutic responsibility and reinforces social isolation.
✘ Confrontation increases anxiety and resistance in a withdrawn client.
✘ Forcing interaction violates autonomy and therapeutic boundaries.
✔ Short, clear sentences with low stimulation reduce cognitive demands and are the appropriate therapeutic approach for withdrawn clients.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client repeatedly complains of pain despite normal findings — most appropriate response?
choices: A. 'I understand your pain is real. Let's manage it together.'
B. 'There is nothing wrong with you.'
questions.rationale: Validating subjective pain while collaborating on management is therapeutic and patient-centered.
choice_rationales: ✔ Acknowledging pain as real (regardless of organic findings) and collaborating on management respects the client's experience and builds trust.
✘ Dismissing the client's experience invalidates their subjective suffering and damages therapeutic rapport.
✘ Ignoring pain complaints is non-therapeutic and violates nursing duty of care.
✘ Telling a client their pain is imaginary is dismissive, stigmatizing, and harmful to the therapeutic relationship.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client develops blindness without organic cause and appears unconcerned — this is?
questions.rationale: Conversion disorder involves unexplained neurological symptoms; 'la belle indifférence' (lack of concern) is characteristic.
choice_rationales: ✘ Illness anxiety disorder involves excessive worry about HAVING a disease, not actual neurological symptoms.
✔ Conversion disorder presents with neurological symptoms (blindness, paralysis) without organic basis; the characteristic indifference distinguishes it.
✘ Factitious disorder involves intentionally producing symptoms; the client is not deliberately creating the blindness.
✘ Malingering involves intentional symptom production for external gain; conversion is unconscious.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which behavior differentiates malingering?
choices: D. Symptoms are intentional for secondary gain
questions.rationale: Malingering is defined by intentional symptom production for external rewards (avoiding work, legal benefits).
choice_rationales: ✘ Unconscious symptom production characterizes conversion disorder and somatic symptom disorder.
✘ Chronicity characterizes somatic symptom disorder but is not the defining feature of malingering.
✘ Severity characterizes the impact of somatic disorders but does not define malingering.
✔ Malingering's defining feature is conscious, intentional symptom fabrication for identifiable external gain.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client preoccupied with having a serious illness despite reassurance — indicates?
questions.rationale: Illness anxiety disorder (formerly hypochondriasis) involves persistent fear of serious disease despite medical reassurance.
choice_rationales: ✔ Illness anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive preoccupation with having an undetected serious illness, persisting despite negative test results.
✘ Conversion disorder involves actual neurological symptoms, not fear of disease.
✘ Pain disorder (somatic symptom disorder with pain) involves actual pain experience, not fear of disease.
✘ Factitious disorder involves intentional symptom production, not health anxiety.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Child avoids eye contact and has repetitive behaviors — most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: ASD is characterized by social communication deficits and restricted, repetitive behaviors.
choice_rationales: ✔ The core features of ASD (DSM-5) are deficits in social communication and restricted/repetitive behaviors; eye contact avoidance is a hallmark.
✘ ADHD presents with hyperactivity, impulsivity, and inattention — not social communication deficits.
✘ Conduct disorder involves violation of others' rights and societal norms; it does not feature repetitive behaviors.
✘ Intellectual disability involves cognitive impairment; repetitive behaviors and social communication deficits specifically indicate ASD.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Child with ADHD cannot sit still — priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Structure reduces overstimulation and provides predictability that helps children with ADHD self-regulate.
choice_rationales: ✘ Isolation increases anxiety and misses the therapeutic opportunity of structured engagement.
✘ Ignoring hyperactive behavior misses the chance to redirect and channel energy appropriately.
✔ Structured environments with clear expectations, routines, and reduced distractions improve attention and behavior in ADHD.
✘ Unrestricted activity without structure exacerbates hyperactivity and inattention.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which finding supports intellectual disability?
choices: B. IQ below 70 with impaired adaptive function
questions.rationale: Intellectual disability requires IQ below 70 AND deficits in adaptive functioning across multiple domains.
choice_rationales: ✘ IQ above 100 is above average; intellectual disability requires IQ below 70.
✔ DSM-5 diagnosis requires IQ below 70 AND significant deficits in adaptive functioning (conceptual, social, practical skills).
✘ Hyperactivity is a feature of ADHD; intellectual disability is diagnosed by IQ and adaptive function.
✘ Good adaptive skills would rule out intellectual disability; deficits in adaptive functioning are required.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Appropriate intervention for autism?
questions.rationale: Consistent routines reduce the anxiety and behavioral distress that ASD children experience with unpredictability.
choice_rationales: ✘ Frequent routine changes cause significant distress in ASD; consistency is therapeutic.
✘ Parental involvement is essential in ASD management; limiting it removes a critical support.
✘ Confrontation increases anxiety and resistance in ASD; gentle, structured guidance is preferred.
✔ Predictable routines reduce anxiety, minimize behavioral meltdowns, and support functional independence in ASD.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Alcohol stopped 8 hours ago — restless, diaphoretic, now visual hallucinations — requires immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Visual hallucinations in alcohol withdrawal indicate delirium tremens — a life-threatening emergency.
choice_rationales: ✔ Visual hallucinations signal progression to delirium tremens (DTs), which carries 5–15% mortality if untreated; immediate benzodiazepine therapy is required.
✘ Mild tremors are expected early withdrawal symptoms manageable with standard monitoring.
✘ Anxiety is a common early withdrawal symptom that does not require immediate emergency intervention.
✘ Increased appetite is not a withdrawal symptom; DTs are characterized by fever, confusion, and hallucinations.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Priority nursing action for suspected opioid overdose?
choices: C. Maintain airway and support breathing
questions.rationale: Opioids cause respiratory depression; airway and breathing are the immediate ABCs priorities.
choice_rationales: ✘ Oral fluids are contraindicated in an unconscious/obtunded patient due to aspiration risk.
✘ Naloxone is essential but administering it without first securing the airway risks aspiration if the patient vomits.
✔ ABCs: airway patency and respiratory support are the immediate life-saving priorities in opioid overdose.
✘ Reassurance is secondary; the patient requires physiological stabilization first.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Disulfiram client states 'I can drink alcohol occasionally' — indicates need for further teaching?
choices: A. 'I will avoid all alcohol-containing products.'
B. 'I may experience flushing if I drink.'
D. 'I will read all medication labels.'
questions.rationale: Disulfiram requires COMPLETE alcohol abstinence; even small amounts cause severe reactions.
choice_rationales: ✘ Avoiding all alcohol products is correct — includes mouthwash, vanilla extract, cough syrup.
✘ Knowing the disulfiram-alcohol reaction (flushing, nausea, palpitations) demonstrates understanding of the risk.
✔ 'Occasionally' is wrong — disulfiram causes a severe acetaldehyde reaction with ANY alcohol intake; total abstinence is required.
✘ Reading labels to avoid hidden alcohol is appropriate and demonstrates understanding.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Best indicator of substance dependence?
choices: B. Increased tolerance and withdrawal symptoms
questions.rationale: Physiological dependence is defined by tolerance (needing more for same effect) and withdrawal (physical symptoms on cessation).
choice_rationales: ✘ Occasional use without consequences may indicate misuse or abuse, not dependence.
✔ Tolerance and withdrawal are the hallmarks of physical dependence, indicating the body has adapted to the substance.
✘ Improved concentration would suggest treatment benefit, not dependence.
✘ Stable work performance contradicts the functional impairment characteristic of substance dependence.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Pinpoint pupils, decreased respirations, drowsiness — nurse prepares which medication?
questions.rationale: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression + drowsiness = opioid toxidrome; naloxone reverses it.
choice_rationales: ✘ Diazepam is a benzodiazepine used for anxiety and alcohol withdrawal; it would worsen opioid-induced respiratory depression.
✘ Methadone is an opioid agonist used for addiction treatment; it would worsen opioid overdose.
✘ Disulfiram is used for alcohol dependence deterrence; it does not reverse opioid toxicity.
✔ Naloxone competitively antagonizes opioid receptors, rapidly reversing respiratory depression, miosis, and sedation.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Primary goal during detoxification?
questions.rationale: Detoxification's immediate goal is safe, medically supervised management of withdrawal.
choice_rationales: ✘ Relapse prevention is a goal of long-term recovery programs, not the acute detoxification phase.
✔ Detoxification safely manages the physiological withdrawal process, preventing life-threatening complications.
✘ Coping skills development occurs in rehabilitation phases, not during acute detoxification.
✘ Long-term abstinence is the ultimate recovery goal; detoxification addresses the immediate medical emergency.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Stimulant user has tachycardia and hyperactivity — this indicates?
questions.rationale: Stimulant intoxication causes CNS hyperactivity: tachycardia, elevated BP, euphoria, and hyperactivity.
choice_rationales: ✘ Stimulant withdrawal causes the opposite: fatigue, depression, and hypersomnia.
✘ Dependence is a chronic pattern; the acute presentation here describes intoxication.
✔ Tachycardia and hyperactivity are classic signs of stimulant (amphetamine, cocaine) intoxication from CNS stimulation.
✘ Detoxification is the medically supervised process of clearing substances; it is not described by the acute signs presented.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Non-modifiable risk factor for substance abuse?
questions.rationale: Genetic factors influencing substance metabolism, reward pathways, and impulse control cannot be changed.
choice_rationales: ✘ Peer influence is a modifiable social risk factor; it can be addressed through counseling and environmental changes.
✘ Cultural norms are modifiable through education and policy; they can shift over time.
✘ Drug accessibility is modifiable through law enforcement, regulations, and environmental design.
✔ Genetic predisposition to addiction cannot be changed; it represents a fixed biological vulnerability.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Alcohol withdrawal seizures — most appropriate medication?
questions.rationale: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam) are the first-line agents for alcohol withdrawal seizures.
choice_rationales: ✔ Benzodiazepines enhance GABA activity, replacing the inhibitory role of alcohol and preventing/treating withdrawal seizures.
✘ Disulfiram is an aversion therapy agent used for maintained sobriety; it has no role in acute withdrawal.
✘ Naltrexone reduces craving and relapse; it is not used for acute withdrawal management.
✘ Acamprosate reduces post-detox craving; it is not used for acute alcohol withdrawal seizures.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Most characteristic finding of dementia?
choices: B. Gradual decline in memory and cognition
questions.rationale: Dementia is characterized by progressive, irreversible cognitive decline over months to years.
choice_rationales: ✘ Sudden onset confusion characterizes delirium, not dementia.
✔ Gradual progressive memory loss and cognitive decline affecting daily function is the hallmark of dementia.
✘ Fluctuating consciousness is a feature of delirium; dementia causes persistent impairment.
✘ Cognitive changes in dementia are generally irreversible; delirium is typically reversible.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Client has acute confusion and hallucinations — most likely condition?
questions.rationale: Delirium has acute onset, fluctuating consciousness, and hallucinations — distinguishing it from dementia.
choice_rationales: ✘ Dementia has insidious gradual onset; acute confusion is not its presentation.
✘ Anxiety disorders cause worry and physiological arousal, not acute confusion and hallucinations.
✘ Depression causes sad mood and cognitive slowing, not acute onset confusion with hallucinations.
✔ Delirium is characterized by acute onset, fluctuating level of consciousness, disorientation, and perceptual disturbances.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Priority intervention for a client with delirium?
choices: C. Ensure safety and prevent injury
questions.rationale: Delirium causes confusion and impaired judgment; safety is the nursing priority.
choice_rationales: ✘ Independence is encouraged in rehabilitation; delirious clients need supervision for safety.
✘ Overstimulation worsens delirium; calm, low-stimulus environments are therapeutic.
✔ Impaired judgment and disorientation in delirium create significant fall and injury risk; safety is the priority.
✘ Limiting interaction is isolating and non-therapeutic; calm, reassuring presence helps orient the client.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Dementia client remembers childhood but forgets recent events — which memory is impaired?
questions.rationale: Early dementia preferentially impairs short-term (recent) memory while remote memories remain intact.
choice_rationales: ✔ Short-term memory involves recent events; hippocampal damage in early Alzheimer's impairs new memory formation while old memories are preserved.
✘ Long-term (remote) memories are relatively preserved in early dementia.
✘ Procedural memory (how to perform skills) is often preserved until late stages of dementia.
✘ Remote memory (distant past events) is typically preserved in early dementia.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Appropriate intervention for disoriented dementia client?
questions.rationale: Gentle, consistent reorientation helps maintain cognitive anchoring and reduce confusion.
choice_rationales: ✘ Excessive stimuli cause agitation and worsen confusion in dementia clients.
✘ Isolation removes social engagement that helps maintain function and quality of life.
✘ Avoiding communication increases isolation and fails to provide orienting cues.
✔ Frequent reorientation (to person, place, time) helps the client maintain contact with reality and reduces anxiety.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Feature that BEST differentiates delirium from dementia?
questions.rationale: Delirium has acute onset (hours to days); dementia develops gradually over months to years.
choice_rationales: ✘ Both delirium and dementia cause memory impairment; onset distinguishes them.
✘ Impaired judgment occurs in both conditions and does not differentiate them.
✔ The acute onset of delirium (hours to days) versus the insidious onset of dementia is the key distinguishing feature.
✘ Confusion is present in both; it alone cannot differentiate the two conditions.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Delirium client with reversed sleep-wake cycle — best intervention?
choices: A. Allow unrestricted sleep during the day
B. Keep the environment dark at all times
questions.rationale: Restoring circadian rhythm through structured daytime activity and nighttime sleep promotes recovery.
choice_rationales: ✘ Unrestricted daytime sleep reinforces the reversal and perpetuates the disruption.
✘ Darkness at all times disrupts circadian cues; natural light exposure during the day helps reset the cycle.
✔ Structured daytime activities, light exposure, and nighttime quiet/darkness restore the sleep-wake cycle and accelerate delirium resolution.
✘ Minimizing all stimulation fails to provide the daytime activity and light cues needed for circadian resetting.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Modifiable risk factor for cognitive disorders?
questions.rationale: Physical inactivity is a lifestyle factor that increases dementia risk and can be modified.
choice_rationales: ✘ Age is the strongest non-modifiable risk factor for dementia; it cannot be changed.
✘ Genetic predisposition (e.g., APOE-ε4) is a non-modifiable risk factor.
✘ Family history reflects genetic and shared environmental risk; it is largely non-modifiable.
✔ Regular physical activity reduces cognitive decline risk; inactivity is modifiable through behavioral intervention.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Purpose of Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE)?
questions.rationale: The MMSE is a standardized tool assessing orientation, memory, attention, language, and visuospatial skills.
choice_rationales: ✘ The MMSE does not diagnose infections; laboratory and clinical assessment are used for that.
✔ The MMSE (0–30 score) quantifies cognitive impairment severity and monitors change over time.
✘ Motor ability is assessed by neurological and physical assessment tools, not the MMSE.
✘ Anxiety is measured by tools like the GAD-7 or Hamilton Anxiety Scale; the MMSE is purely cognitive.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Delirious client attempts to remove IV lines — priority action?
choices: D. Reorient and provide calm environment
questions.rationale: Non-pharmacological reorientation and de-escalation are the first-line approach before restraints.
choice_rationales: ✘ Restraints are a last resort; they can worsen agitation, cause injury, and increase mortality in delirium.
✘ Sedatives should be used cautiously; non-pharmacological measures come first.
✘ Ignoring the behavior risks patient safety — the IV line must be protected.
✔ Calm reassurance, gentle reorientation, and removing the stimulus (repositioning) are first-line before any restraint or medication.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Common characteristic of ALL personality disorders?
choices: D. Complete loss of reality testing
questions.rationale: Personality disorders are defined by enduring, inflexible patterns that deviate from cultural norms.
choice_rationales: ✔ Personality disorders share chronicity and inflexibility as defining features; they begin in adolescence/early adulthood and persist throughout life.
✘ Hallucinations are psychotic features characteristic of schizophrenia, not personality disorders.
✘ Personality disorders have insidious onset; sudden onset is not a defining feature.
✘ Most personality disorder clients maintain reality testing; psychosis is not a defining feature.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Personality disorders are typically diagnosed when symptoms?
choices: B. Are stable since adolescence/early adulthood and pervasive
D. Are associated with medical illness
questions.rationale: DSM-5 requires onset in adolescence/early adulthood, stability over time, and pervasiveness across situations.
choice_rationales: ✘ Late adulthood onset is not characteristic; personality disorders begin early in life.
✔ Long-standing (from adolescence), pervasive (across multiple contexts), and inflexible patterns are the defining criteria.
✘ Stress-only manifestation describes adjustment disorders; personality disorders are pervasive and context-independent.
✘ Medical illness association does not define personality disorders; they are psychological patterns.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Cluster A personality disorders described as odd or eccentric includes?
questions.rationale: Cluster A (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) features odd, eccentric thinking and behavior.
choice_rationales: ✘ Cluster B includes antisocial, borderline, histrionic, and narcissistic — dramatic and erratic, not odd.
✘ Cluster C includes avoidant, dependent, and OCPD — anxious and fearful, not eccentric.
✘ There is no Cluster D in the DSM-5 personality disorder classification.
✔ Cluster A personality disorders feature unusual thinking patterns and behaviors that appear odd or eccentric to others.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Socially detached client with limited emotional expression is MOST consistent with?
questions.rationale: Schizoid PD involves pervasive detachment from social relationships and restricted emotional expression.
choice_rationales: ✘ Paranoid PD features distrust and suspicion; emotional range may be limited but the primary feature is suspicion, not detachment.
✔ Schizoid PD is characterized by preference for solitary activities, emotional coldness, and indifference to social relationships.
✘ Histrionic PD features excessive emotionality and attention-seeking — the opposite of emotional restriction.
✘ Dependent PD features excessive reliance on others and fear of abandonment, not emotional detachment.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Which disorder is characterized by pervasive distrust and suspicion?
questions.rationale: Paranoid PD involves persistent distrust and suspicion without adequate basis.
choice_rationales: ✘ Avoidant PD involves social inhibition from fear of rejection, not distrust of others' motives.
✘ Narcissistic PD involves grandiosity and lack of empathy, not pervasive suspicion.
✔ Paranoid PD is defined by pervasive, unfounded suspicion that others are exploiting, deceiving, or harming the client.
✘ Borderline PD involves emotional dysregulation and unstable relationships, not primarily suspicion.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Cluster B personality disorder client MOST likely demonstrates?
questions.rationale: Cluster B disorders (antisocial, borderline, histrionic, narcissistic) are dramatic, emotional, and erratic.
choice_rationales: ✔ Cluster B is the 'dramatic, emotional, erratic' cluster — all four disorders feature intense emotional expression and unpredictable behavior.
✘ Anxiety and fearfulness characterize Cluster C disorders (avoidant, dependent, OCPD).
✘ Social withdrawal is characteristic of Cluster A schizoid personality disorder.
✘ Perfectionism is a feature of OCPD in Cluster C, not Cluster B.

questions.subject: Psychiatric Nursing
questions.stem: Key feature of borderline personality disorder?
choices: D. Instability in relationships and self-image
questions.rationale: BPD is defined by emotional dysregulation, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, and impulsivity.
choice_rationales: ✘ Lack of emotional expression characterizes schizoid PD; BPD features intense emotional dysregulation.
